chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 CRU International Limited confidential Executive summary Silver: prices will remain strong until 2015 Although the ebb and flow in ETF holdings are no longer providing a demonstrable guide to investors’ desire to buy, our tabulations show that a fundamental surplus of 5,500-6,000 t/y is being generated without putting any immense downward pressure on silver’s price. This latent demand will hold until monetary policy is tightened in 2015. Silver will then fall to $15/oz. • After bouncing back in 2010, fabrication demand has slipped once again in 2011-12. It is forecast to grow at 3-3.5% p.a. in 2013-15 but weaker coinage will be a drag beyond then. • Subject to financing being available, project development would largely be unencumbered at today’s prices. However, mine supply will be curtailed in 2016-17 once prices have fallen. • Even though fabrication demand will struggle to grow in 2016-17 as coinage offtake falls, the price-induced curtailments will rein in mine supply by more and cut the market’s surplus to around 3,000 tonnes in 2017. The stock-to-consumption ratio will then be 2.5-2.6 years.

2 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Executive Summary CRU International Limited confidential Other precious metal markets Gold The gold market is expected to remain in fundamental surplus over the next five years. After peaking in 2013, the surplus may then decline during the 2014-17 period. • The world’s gold supplies were largely unchanged in the first half of 2012 as a marginal rise in primary gold supply was largely offset by the fall in supply generated by gold recycling programmes. • Central banks continue to diversify their foreign exchange reserves into gold but their interest has started to wane. • Fabrication demand, jewellery in particular, remains weak but is set to improve in later years on the back of lower prices and rising incomes in many key developing countries. Platinum The platinum market surplus is shrinking fast as troubles with supply deepen. The return to a nearly balanced market is now expected in 2014 but it is unlikely that the fundamental balance will shift into deficit at any point in our forecast period.

balanced market is now expected in 2014 but it is unlikely that the fundamental balance will shift into deficit at any point in our forecast period. • The decline in platinum mine production is expected to continue in 2013 based in part on the decisions made in 2012 to curtail or defer expansion plans. • Demand from global car manufacturers is expected to strengthen next year thanks to the introduction of Euro VI emissions rules for new vehicles coming into effect on January 1st 2013. • During 2014-17, both supply and demand are likely to grow at approximately the same rate, driven by the new mining projects coming on stream and improved demand on the back of recovering global economy. This should prevent the market from switching into deficit. Palladium Following a significant surplus posted in 2011, the palladium market is set to move into fundamental deficit this year. The deficit is expected to continue widening until the end of our outlook period. • Supply issues in South Africa are having a significant impact on the palladium market. To reflect these developments our outlook for mine output has undergone a major cut.

To reflect these developments our outlook for mine output has undergone a major cut. • Palladium consumption continues to benefit from firm demand from the global automotive industry, particularly from automakers in the US market. • So far this year, shipments of palladium from the Russian stockpile have been moderate, reinforcing our view that sales from this source may be drying up.

Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 CRU International Limited confidential Chapter 1 Silver 1. Summary The market has been continuing to generate a fundamental surplus of 5,000-6,000 t/y in 2011-12 with investment demand ensuring that silver’s price remains largely supported at around $30/oz. We believe that investors’ appetite for buying silver will not wane until they view other investment options more favourably. Economic conditions are unlikely to support this until 2015 at which point monetary policy is forecast to be tightened. Silver’s price is forecast to fall towards $15/oz in 2016-17 and this should be sufficient to begin curtailing mine supply. The market’s surplus will shrink to around 3,000 tonnes in 2017 as a result.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 8 2. Fabrication demand 2.1 Summary After bouncing back by 10.4% (or 3,130 tonnes) to 26,940 tonnes in 2010 following the 10.4% drop resulting from the Global Financial Crisis, global fabrication demand is estimated to have slipped by 1.2% (or 320 tonnes). Photographic offtake may have fallen by its smallest absolute rate since 2001 as a result of the saturation of digital technology in the basic consumer photography market, but industrial demand went back into reverse again as well. Demand in 2011 would have fallen much further if silver’s use in coins and medals had not jumped by a further 19.1%. Indeed, excluding coinage, it fell by 3.8% (or 910 tonnes) to 22,940 tonnes. We have global fabrication slipping a little further in 2012. Industrial demand has weakened again as a result of an economic slowdown extending to all regions of the world, but we expect photographic offtake to slip even more slowly and silver’s use in jewellery to just about hold up.

relations.evidence: Fabrication demand for silver

chunk.text: tending to all regions of the world, but we expect photographic offtake to slip even more slowly and silver’s use in jewellery to just about hold up. However, although we have all this slipping by just 0.7% (or about 150 tonnes), we also have total fabrication contracting by 0.7% (or about 200 tonnes) because we have not seen any further growth in coinage. Nevertheless, coinage still accounts for 14% of all fabrication. Silver’s use in coinage is unlikely to remain elevated when the economy finally recovers and investor interest wanes. However, this will coincide with strong growth in industrial demand, and electronics offtake in particular. Photographic offtake may still be in decline but losses will be modest and we therefore still have the total fabrication rising by 3,275 tonnes in 2012-17 to 29,700 tonnes. This is over 2,000 tonnes higher than the peak in 2000 of 27,640 tonnes. Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 9 2.2 Jewellery & Silverware Demand for silver for use in jewellery and silverware decreased by 4.5% (or 300 tonnes) to 6,420 tonnes in 2011. Having increased by 1.8% (or 120 tonnes) between 2008 and 2010, it has therefore more than offset all of its post-Global Financial Crisis gains. Moreover, offtake in 2011 was at its lowest level since 1991 when it totalled just 5,960 tonnes, and it was 26.4% (or 2,300 tonnes) below its peak in 2001 when the world economy was in the midst of an economic slowdown. World GDP in 2001 grew by just 1.4%, a rate which was matched in 2008. There is clearly no strong link merely between this area of demand and wider economic conditions. Silver’s use in jewellery and silverware is largely its most price-elastic area of fabrication demand. This stems from two main drivers, plus a third lesser influence that have been in action. To those buyers that consider jewellery and silverware to be discretionary items, silver’s price and the fortunes of the wider economy will have an impact on their willingness to buy.

entities.name_en: use of silver in jewellery and silverware

relations.evidence: for use in jewellery and silverware

chunk.text: llery and silverware to be discretionary items, silver’s price and the fortunes of the wider economy will have an impact on their willingness to buy. Of course, fabrication costs must be considered too, and these can be considerable relative to the price of silver. However, all other things being equal, price will be an increasingly important driver as it rises. Meanwhile, a squeeze on households’ incomes, or at least the fear of this, has an influence on their ability or willingness to buy silver jewellery and silverware. The increase in silver’s price from 2004 has had a clear impact on its use in jewellery and silverware through to 2008. Italy has historically been at the centre of this activity in Europe, and a gradual rising price has correlated with a steady decline in European jewellery offtake. However, by far the greatest losses during this period were experienced in India where many purchases of silver jewellery and silverware act as a store of wealth alongside gold. There is evidence that Indian offtake is influenced by the relative prices of silver and gold, with demand for silver losing out when its price strengthens relative to gold as it did in 2004-08.

relations.evidence: silver’s price and the fortunes of the wider economy will have an impact on their willingness to buy. Of course, fabrication costs must be considered too, and these can be considerable relative to the price of silver.

chunk.text: uenced by the relative prices of silver and gold, with demand for silver losing out when its price strengthens relative to gold as it did in 2004-08. On the other hand, as agricultural incomes rise in India, gold become increasingly affordable. A combination of silver’s price pushing even higher and the spreading of renewed economic weakness has been weighing heavily on silver’s use in jewellery and silverware in 2011-12. Prices are 50-60% higher than their average in 2010 and no region is immune to at least some economic anxieties. Europe is in the vanguard in respect to deteriorating economic conditions with its GDP having failed to fully recover to its level in 2008 before slipping once again in 2012. Italy’s silver jewellery market has suffered significantly and this has resulted in Europe’s offtake falling by 19% (or 310 tonnes) to 1,340 tonnes in 2011. We have it coming off by another 10% (or 130 tonnes) in 2012 so that it stands at just 43% of its peak level in 2000. Going forward we expect silver’s use in jewellery and silverware to continue to be guided by its price as well as wider economic fortunes.

chunk.text: l in 2000. Going forward we expect silver’s use in jewellery and silverware to continue to be guided by its price as well as wider economic fortunes. European GDP is only forecast to fully recover all of what it has lost since the Global Financial Crisis in 2014. Growth in global GDP is forecast to re-accelerate through 2013-14 but demand for silver jewellery and silverware is unlikely to recover much just yet if silver’s price is still at about $30/oz. China will be the key driver at this time and, so long as demand elsewhere does not deteriorate much further, we believe that the global total will manage to fully recover its losses since 2010 and total 7,000-7,100 tonnes in 2015. Of course, this still leaves the total 19% (or about 1,650 tonnes) below its high in 2001. However, so long as silver’s price then retreats towards $15/t in 2016-17, we have demand bouncing back to almost 7,800 tonnes in 2017. This would mean that silver’ use in jewellery and silverware will have recovered 60% of all its peak-to-trough fall. The precise extent of this

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 14 will be largely influenced by the path taken by offtake in India where a number of competing factors will be in play. A lower price would certainly make it more affordable, with consumers able to increase the volume bought at little extra cost. Any increase in the gold/silver price ratio also has the potential to be positive for silver. On the other hand, so long as household incomes continue to rise in India, gold will be increasingly accessible. There is also the theory of gold being a Giffen good. Silver would then lose out even if gold becomes relatively stronger. 2.3 Photography Silver’s use in photography fell for the twelfth consecutive year in 2011, declining by 7.3% (or 160 tonnes) to 2,040 tonnes. Offtake therefore stood at just 29% of, or 5,060 tonnes below, its peak in 1999 of 7,100 tonnes. The extent of the decline in silver’s use in photography is also notable. A 7.3% decrease is the smallest drop since 2004 when offtake still stood at 79% of its peak in 1999. Moreover, the absolute fall of 160 tonnes in 2011 was the smallest since 2001 which was just the second year of decline in silver’s use in this sector.

relations.evidence: Silver’s use in photography fell for the twelfth consecutive year in 2011, declining by 7.3% (or 160 tonnes) to 2,040 tonnes.

chunk.text: reover, the absolute fall of 160 tonnes in 2011 was the smallest since 2001 which was just the second year of decline in silver’s use in this sector. The sharp slowdown in the erosion of photographic offtake was expected although the precise timing of this transition has been difficult to predict. A number of factors have contributed to this development. The primary driver behind the significant decline in photographic offtake in the 2000s and more recent easing in the rate of contraction is basic photography. Demand fell rapidly as the result of the adoption of digital technology and a point was always going to be approached when it saturated the basic photography market. It was never going to fall below zero, and there are amateur users that are not adopting digital technology and these are beginning to support some residual demand for consumer film. This phenomenon helps to explain why the rate of fall in photographic demand slowed substantially in 2011. We would not even rule out the possibility of silver’s use in basic photography beginning to recover. There is evidence of this in Japan.

relations.evidence: silver’s use in basic photography

chunk.text: ly in 2011. We would not even rule out the possibility of silver’s use in basic photography beginning to recover. There is evidence of this in Japan. Furthermore, the printing of traditional photographs is still quite a popular choice among digital photographers and so demand for photographic paper may hold up better. While there is the distinct possibility that silver’s use in basic photography could eventually turn, the medical sector will continue to be a source of fabrication losses. However, we doubt that this trend will be rapid, particularly in the short term. The trend between silver demand and the strength of the economy is not always positive. Today’s austerity measures will be cutting investment budgets and, to this end, the take up of digital radiography equipment will surely slow. The rate at which silver’s use in all photographic applications declines should therefore remain moderate at just 3-3.5% through to 2017. This will leave overall photographic offtake at 1,660 tonnes in 2017, a level that is 77% (or 5,440 tonnes) below its peak in 1999.

chunk.text: through to 2017. This will leave overall photographic offtake at 1,660 tonnes in 2017, a level that is 77% (or 5,440 tonnes) below its peak in 1999. 2.4 Electrical & Electronics Demand for silver for use in electronics has been vital for supporting overall fabrication and is forecast to play a crucial role in future. Electronics offtake is now estimated to have bounced by 35% in 2010 following the 18% fall resulting from the Global Financial Crisis in 2009. The aggressive restocking along the supply chain in 2010 has nevertheless given way, and silver’s use in electrical and electronics items is estimated to have contracted by 3.2% (or 250 tonnes) to 7,470 tonnes in 2011, primarily as a result of disruption to Japanese manufacturing. Despite this, electronics fabrication is still comfortably above pre-crisis levels.

relations.evidence: for use in electronics has been vital for supporting overall fabrication and is forecast to play a crucial role in future.

chunk.text: years. Global economic conditions have continued to be a drag on electronics offtake in 2012 and we have the global total again falling by about 3%. However, we still believe that silver’s use in electronics will be the principal driver of total fabrication demand going forward, and have electronics demand rising by about 2,175 tonnes to almost 9,425 tonnes in 2017. 2.5 Other fabrication We continue to believe that the recovery in demand from silver’s other industrial uses was less convincing than for electronics. However, offtake stemming from brazing alloys and solders did manage to rise by another 3.6% (or 80 tonnes) to 2,310 tonnes in 2011 so that it more than recovered what it lost during the Global Financial Crisis. The same cannot be said for silver’s other residual industrial uses where offtake is estimated to have fallen by 5.6% (or 280 tonnes) to 4,700 tonnes. Demand for silver from these other uses will nevertheless grow more convincingly once the global economy picks up meaningfully in 2013-14 as forecast.

relations.evidence: silver’s use in electronics will be the principal driver of total fabrication demand going forward

chunk.text: emand for silver from these other uses will nevertheless grow more convincingly once the global economy picks up meaningfully in 2013-14 as forecast. In contrast to all other areas of fabrication demand, silver’s use in coins and medals swelled even further in 2011, increasing by 19% (or 590 tonnes) to 3,680 tonnes. We continue to have this demand collapsing at the same time as rising interest rates erode investor interest in silver.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 16 3. Supply from recycled scrap 3.1 Summary The supply of silver from recycled scrap was reasonably stable at about 6,000 t/y through to 2009. This perhaps appears surprising given that supply from photographic scrap, which had accounted for about 70% of the total at the turn of the century, had fallen by 55-60% by then. However, alongside a steady rise in supply from scrapped catalysts stemming from expanding fabrication, an almost entirely new source of scrap supply was emerging in the form of silver contained in electrical and electronic products. Moreover, silver’s rising price has led to the emergence of price-sensitive supply such as scrapped silverware. The severity of the economic downturn during the Global Financial Crisis was such that it had significant repercussions for the supply of silver from recycled scrap. The sharp contraction in industrial demand largely stemmed from the deferral of replacement units as companies and households sought to cut their outgoings to the bone. The performance of older catalysts may deteriorate but this was considered a price worth paying in some instance.

relations.evidence: supply of silver from recycled scrap

They show that semiconductor shipments in the first half of 2012 fell by 5% year on year. This decline would have been more severe if the second-quarter drop had not been limited by an already weak Japanese performance in 2011 Q2 as a result of the fall-out of the Tohoku earthquake. Of course, losses have been concentrated in the developed world but the rapid growth in the Asia Pacific region has also abated with shipments having been roughly flat for the last two years. Global economic conditions have continued to be a drag on electronics offtake in 2012 and we have the global total again falling by about 3%.

Consumer paper is a much more significant source of silver that can be recovered although we believe that supply from here will not fall much further as we believe that demand for consumer paper has not much further to fall. Scrap stemming from radiography is therefore now more important, particularly in respect to capital scrap which is the second source of scrap. This has much further to fall although the financial constraints on the public sector will limit the rate at which it takes up digital technology. We have the supply of silver recovered from recycled photographic scrap falling by 7.8% (or 123 tonnes) to 1,451 tonnes in 2011, the twelfth consecutive year of decline. This is just a third of, or 2,888 tonnes below, its peak in 2009 of 4,339 tonnes. As with photographic offtake itself, the rate of decline in these scrap supplies is moderating although the lag between fabrication and scrapping for capital scrap in radiography results in supply declining at a somewhat higher rate.

chunk.text: North America, but increasingly in China too. However, collection rates have also been aided by the attraction of the high value of the scrap metal. A further consideration in relation to the availability of electronics scrap is the lag between when an electrical/electronic product is made and scrapped. This lag of 3-5 years is also influenced by the economic cycle because delays to replacing an electronic product cuts scrap availability as well as fabrication. This was acute during the Global Financial Crisis. The growth in the supply of silver from recycled electrical/electronic scrap slowed to only 4.8% (or 64 tonnes) in 2009 because there was a fall in products being replaced and scrapped. This ultimately led to significant pent-up demand being unleashed in 2010 when there was a sharp increase of 33.9% (or 477 tonnes). Growth has slowed to 11% in 2011 as this pent-up demand dissipates and more recent economic weakness has again cut short-term gains. 3.4 Other There was significant pent-up demand for replacement catalysts in 2010 but, with this having been met, silver supply from recycled catalysts has since fallen by 10.7% (or 181 tonnes) to 1,513 tonnes in 2011.

Meanwhile, we estimate that supply from other more price-sensitive sources of scrap ballooned by about 50% (or almost 1,000 tonnes) as silver’s price spiked to almost $50/oz. Such supply has since been falling as a result of there being a much smaller pool of potential scrap available.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 21 4. Mine production 4.1 Summary Before making any allowance for disruption, global mine supply is projected to rise by 3% (or about 750 tonnes) to almost 25,200 tonnes. The re-acceleration of growth follows losses at a number of major mines in 2011. We have mine output in 2012 being boosted by notable gains at thirteen mines with these adding 578 tonnes. There are also seven notable losses that will cost 313 tonnes. However, many smaller incremental gains will add almost another 500 tonnes. The global total stands at 25,000 tonnes once an allowance for disruption is made. Committed Western mine out is set to rise by 750 tonnes in 2012-17 to 18,750 tonnes with it peaking at 19,325 tonnes in 2016 when Pascua-Lama should by then be running at full capacity. Firm additions from new and redeveloping mines will add almost 2,500 tonnes. Losses from contractions and exhaustions will cost a little over 2,000 tonnes. The difference stems from the net gains from other existing mines. However, today’s firm mines are not the only potential future suppliers.

relations.evidence: global mine supply is projected to rise by 3% (or about 750 tonnes) to almost 25,200 tonnes.

chunk.text: 0 tonnes. The difference stems from the net gains from other existing mines. However, today’s firm mines are not the only potential future suppliers. We believe that 33 as-yet uncommitted Western projects have some chance of supplying before the end of 2017. While there is a negligible possibility that they will all proceed as they hope, they could theoretically add 3,175 tonnes to Western supply in 2012-17. We therefore have potential Western production totalling 21,925 tonnes in 2017 and this is 3,900 tonnes greater than in 2012. It will in fact peak at just shy of 22,000 tonnes in 2016. In addition to Pascua-Lama’s contribution peaking, this is when the proponents of a string of other less certain projects are hoping to be operating fully and it is before a wave of closures in 2017. On a global level, mine production could potentially hit 29,500 tonnes in 2017 with a peak of 29,575 tonnes in 2016. Total potential growth therefore stands at 4,575 tonnes.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 22 4.2 Recent developments Global mine production increased by 1.6% (or 384 tonnes) to 24,431 tonnes in 2011. This is the smallest percentage gain since a previous 1.6% increase in 2008 and the smallest absolute rise since 2006. Growth can largely be attributed to developments in China and Kazakhstan. Indeed, we have output in the former Western world slipping by 0.6% (or 100 tonnes). Our Chinese figures are admittedly the least accurate component of our analysis but, even though we know that official figures for lead and zinc mine production are significantly overstated, strong growth in China is consistent with activity within this country’s base metals mining sector. Losses elsewhere in 2011 were significant. Mine output fell in the world’s second- and fourth largest silver mining countries. Output fell in Peru with losses led by Antamina and the Hochschild mines. Meanwhile, sharply lower grades at Cannington slashed Australian output. This is still the world’s largest silver mine because output at Fresnillo also fell sharply as a result of a fall in head grades.

relations.evidence: Global mine production increased by 1.6% (or 384 tonnes) to 24,431 tonnes in 2011.

chunk.text: Australian output. This is still the world’s largest silver mine because output at Fresnillo also fell sharply as a result of a fall in head grades. Nevertheless, Mexican output was still able to grow thanks to gains stemming from Peñasquito, Saucito, Palmarejo, El Aguila, Dolores and Cananea. There has been growth elsewhere too. For example, Sindesar Khurd has been adding to Indian output. Before including an allowance for disruption, mine production in 2012 is set to expand by 3.1% (or 757 tonnes) to 25,188 tonnes. This will be the result of further growth in Mexico and Kazakhstan, as well as (generally partial) recoveries in supply from Peru and Indonesia. Output will also continue to grow in India, as a result of Sindesar Khurd, as well as gains at most of Sweden’s mines. Canadian output has also been growing with Bellekeno and, more recently, Wolverine, continuing to ramp up. In contrast, US output is continuing to decline. Output at both of Hecla’s mines declined in 2011, with Lucky Friday having been idle in 2012.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 25 Growth in Mexican mine output of 425 tonnes in 2011 is half of 2010’s increase Mine production in North America increased by almost 300 tonnes to about 6,600 tonnes in 2011. Given that Mexico added 425 tonnes to take its output to 4,924 tonnes, Canada and the USA must have together experienced a fall in production. As it turns out, we estimate that Canadian supplies increased by 62 tonnes. This largely stems from a first full year of operations at Bellekeno, while Wolverine produced more despite its slow ramp-up. We should also point out that Malartic was commissioned during 2011. Losses in the USA have therefore totalled almost 200 tonnes with Hecla’s two mines – Greens Creek and Lucky Friday – accounting for 76 tonnes. Output also fell at Bingham Canyon, Rochester and Mission. The single-largest addition to supply in Mexico was Peñasquito which added 158 tonnes as it continued to ramp up towards full production. Coming a close second to this was Saucito where a first full year of operations added 146 tonnes but this was more than offset for Fresnillo by lower production at its namesake mine.

North American mine production in 2012 is projected to increase by only little more than 100 tonnes to just in excess of 6,700 tonnes, and this is before making an allowance for disruption. Mexican supply is set to rise by only 65 tonnes.

chunk.text: ndoubtedly one of the least reliable elements of our mine-side analysis because we have little knowledge about the universe of mines in that country. Although we track a number of the country’s largest silver-bearing mines, some of which are in fact base metal mines, we still have too incomplete a picture to reliably determine changes in overall output. For this reason, we seek to tally our estimate with our view of other market variables and assume that all disparities offset each other. We first contrast our view of China’s consumption with likely scrap availability, its net trade with the rest of the world, and anecdotal evidence of stock movements. This gives us a first pass estimate of the level of primary supplies. We then contrast this with our assessment of the silver content of the concentrates (and other feed) imported by China’s smelters. Of course, we do not pretend that this can be accurately gauged. We believe that we have a good idea of the silver content of the base metal concentrates produced in the West’s mines, but we clearly do not know exactly what quantity of each Western mine’s concentrates are being imported by China’s smelters.

We have only very limited growth in China, although this is highly uncertain. Official data show lead and zinc mining growing very strongly. However, we do know that the figures are overstated. Kazakh supplies should also increase if mill throughput can recover at Zhezkazgan. Meanwhile, Indian supplies are continuing to be boosted by increased supplies from Sindesar Khurd, although this is being partly offset by reduced tonnages from Rampura Agucha. Indonesian supplies should also increase so long as Grasberg’s mill throughput recovers. Elsewhere, we have Polymetal producing 62 tonnes more, with this coming from Khakanja as well as Dukat, although this addition to Russian output will be partly offset by reduced supplies from Kupol. Sweden’s output is also expected to increase by 51 tonnes with this being attributable to developments at all four of its silver-bearing mines.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 29 4.3 Mine production costs Our analysis focuses on miners’ pro-rata costs. Although many miners prefer to report their net of by-product costs, we believe that pro-rata costing is a more useful tool for our analysis. We have looked at the costs of 141 mines in 2011 and these had a combined output of almost 16,000 tonnes (65% of the global total). Of these mines, 86 had negative net of by-product costs. The other 56 mines (40% of those costed) had positive net of by-product costs, and these produced 7,761 tonnes, which is equivalent to 49% of the total production costed. Silver’s average price in 2011 was $35.12/oz. This meant that no mine that we costed made a loss in that year, although two mines – SIMSA’s San Vicente and Hudbay’s Snow Lake – only managed to break even. This said, these two mines produce barely any silver. The average pro-rata cost was $16.05/oz and this means that half of the silver costed was produced making a profit of at least $19/oz or 119%. In fact 63% of the production costed made a profit of at least 100% while three-quarters of output had costs of less than $19.50/oz and therefore made profits of at least 80%.

relations.evidence: Silver’s average price in 2011 was $35.12/oz.

chunk.text: ion costed made a profit of at least 100% while three-quarters of output had costs of less than $19.50/oz and therefore made profits of at least 80%. The 90th percentile producer had costs of $24.50/oz and had a 44% margin. The price of zinc, lead, copper, gold and silver increased in 2011 but silver’s average rose by a far greater percentage than any other metal. It therefore assumed a much greater share of miners’ costs. Silver has generally held on to this relative strength by slipping by less than zinc, lead and copper, although gold has lifted further this year. Based on miners’ cost per tonne of ore in 2011, as well as today’s prices, TC/RCs and exchange rates, the 90th percentile producer has a pro-rata cost that is $10/oz less than today’s price of $34/oz, while the average producer was making a profit of 125%. Other things being equal, silver would have to fall to just $11/oz to push the 90th percentile producer into loss. Lower by-product prices would be more harmful.

We have closures through exhaustion amounting to almost 1,300 tonnes, while losses stemming from major contractions will cost more than another 725 tonnes.

chunk.text: e closures through exhaustion amounting to almost 1,300 tonnes, while losses stemming from major contractions will cost more than another 725 tonnes. Together, this gives us production losses of roughly 2,025 tonnes by 2017. For firm Western output to increase by 750 tonnes in 2012-17, additions to supply must add up to 2,775 tonnes. Almost 2,125 tonnes will come from new mines, while redevelopments will add up to another 350 tonnes. Although we can identify major expansions that will add over 1,000 tonnes, net expansions will be trimmed by a large number of small incremental losses so that they total less than 325 tonnes. All regions will see output grow with the exception of Australia. There are no developments that are significant enough to offset the fall in head grades at Cannington. Elsewhere, despite the recent losses in South America, the emergence of Pascua-Lama as the world’s largest silver miner is helping to boost this region’s output by most. Additions to supply in Mexico are also in the pipeline, including further growth at Peñasquito. This will boost North American supply.

With firm losses emerging in 2016-17, Western potential will actually peak in 2016 at 22,150 tonnes. There is therefore the potential for Western production in 2016 to be 4,125 tonnes higher than in 2012, and still up by 3,900 tonnes even in 2017. Potential new mines account for 87% (or 2,775 tonnes) of all uncommitted Western developments. chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 34 Firm Western mine output peaks in 2016 15.8 16.4 17.0 17.6 18.2 18.8 19.4 20.0 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 committed Western mine output; '000 tonnes silver New Mines Total Committed Redevelopments Existing Mines less Closures/Contractions Net Expansions  • New mines – There are fifteen Western mines that have recently come on stream or are certain to be commissioned between now and the end of 2017. Together they are set to add over 2,100 tonnes to annual Western supply in 2012-17. Six mines were already supplying by the end of 2011 and they are still set to add 234 tonnes. Even when another six mines that will have supplied by the end of 2012 are included, the combined addition to annual Western supply by 2017 is still only 481 tonnes. There is therefore still much more to come. There are only three committed new mines that are due to be commissioned beyond the end of 2012 and two of these mines rank as the most significant firm developments in the West. Tahoe is set to bring its Escobal mine in Guatemala on stream in 2013 and this operation will ultimately produce 650 t/y.

relations.evidence: Western mine output peaks in 2016
There are fifteen Western mines that have recently come on stream or are certain to be commissioned between now and the end of 2017.

chunk.text: m developments in the West. Tahoe is set to bring its Escobal mine in Guatemala on stream in 2013 and this operation will ultimately produce 650 t/y. This will make it no less than the fourth largest Western miner. On the coattails of this, Barrick Gold will commission its Pascua-Lama project in Chile in 2014 and, by producing an average of 1,025 t/y when it reaches full production, this mine will more than likely be the world’s largest silver miner. Peñasquito may take the top spot in some years. Silver is a by-product to gold at both these mines. Pirquitas has been having a relatively swift ramp-up to full production. In contrast, Wolverine still has some way to go before it comes close to reaching its capacity. At the same time, many other mines that are operating in 2011-12 will only supply a modest fraction of what they will ultimately achieve. To this end, our tabulations have the twelve mines that will supply by the end of 2012 adding an extra 259 tonnes to Western supply in 2012. With Pascua-Lama not supplying until 2014, the extra contribution of new mines in 2013 will still amount to only 279 tonnes.

Other new mines could well have become committed and supply in 2017 but today’s tally of firm new mines will not be in a position to lift Western output much more in that year. • Redevelopments – We list eleven mines as redevelopments. This term describes mines that are being reactivated after having been forced to suspend operations at an earlier date or where production rates were partially restricted. A curtailment could have been the result of technical or labour relations difficulties, or prompted by a period of low prices. All but one of the mines will have supplied by the end of 2012 and seven mines were already producing in 2011. El Cubo’s output is still recovering following on from industrial action that ended in mid-2011, while Primero is still hoping to return mill throughout at San Dimas to past norms.

Golden Grove has spent 2012 processing more of its copper ores and it will ultimately increase the proportion of its zinc-silver ores in the mix in the coming years. Similarly, while we now believe that mill throughput at Almagrera (Aguas Teñidas) has reached full capacity, we continue to rank this mine as a redevelopment as this mine processes more of its zinc-silver ores. It is a similar story at Neves-Corvo. All the other mines listed as redevelopments are operations that are being reactivated. Dikulushi and Santa Eulalia were producing before the end of 2011. Zinc, if not silver, was being produced at Langlois too by then. Twin Hills and the Rio Tinto Copper mines could also be supplying by the end of 2012. However, the single-largest addition to Western supply by way of a redevelopment is Lucky Friday which will ultimately add 146 tonnes. This mine was suspended in late 2011 following fatalities and technical issues. It should be brought back on stream in 2013.

We count thirteen operations that will contribute at least an extra 20 t/y, either as a result of a specific mill expansion or a natural increase in head grades, and together these should add over 1,000 tonnes to committed Western supply in 2012-17. chunk.text: pting operations, the total quantity of ore treated is expected to grow by only 11% in 2012-17. In contrast, head grades are forecast to rise by 42%. The two other largest expansions that we list – Mt Isa and Garpenberg – are the result of specific mill expansions. Meanwhile, Cerro de Pasco’s mill throughput is being restored to past rates as a result of it introducing silver-ores from 2013. We regard this development as an expansion rather than a redevelopment because we have its head grades increasing by a greater percentage than the rise in its mill throughput. The expansions of the other operations listed stem from both mill expansions and higher head grades. Mill expansions explain the inclusion of Antamina, Cerro Lindo and Rampura Agucha. This will be the second expansion for Cerro Lindo. The expansions at all of the other mines stem from projected increases in head grades. • Major contractions – There are rarely cases of existing mines taking the decision to cut mill throughput on a permanent basis, and all six of the mines at which we believe output will fall by at least 20 t/y between 2012 and 2017 are projected to instead encounter a natural decline in head grades.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 40 5. Investor demand, prices and market balance 5.1 Investor demand We are still aware of thirteen exchange-traded funds (ETFs) in which silver is either the whole, majority, or minority constituent. This includes the Central Fund of Canada. There has therefore been no change in the number of funds during the last year although we would concede that one or more could lie beneath our radar. However, any ETF that we have missed is unlikely to be significant in comparison with Blackrock’s iShares. Indeed, this ETF provider took over Canada’s Claymore Investments and has added 115 tonnes to the circa 9,800 tonnes that it was previously managing. The lion’s share of this is held in its giant US-based fund. ETF holdings peaked at over 18,500 tonnes in March-April 2011 when silver rallied to up to a high of around $50/oz. They subsequently fell to less than 17,000 tonnes in the subsequent sell-off and, despite rallying once again to a slightly lower peak in August 2011, ETF holdings remained well below 18,000 tonnes.

chunk.text: in the subsequent sell-off and, despite rallying once again to a slightly lower peak in August 2011, ETF holdings remained well below 18,000 tonnes. And, with silver’s price having generally been lower during the subsequent twelve months, ETF holdings have only made fleeting jumps above 18,000 tonnes. However, holdings are at a 19-month higher alongside silver’s recent rally. Although investors’ uptake of ETFs is clearly positive for silver’s price, an absence has not resulted in the removal of all price support. Investors appear to have found new investment channels and our tabulations point to other private holdings rising by over 10,000 tonnes in 2010-11. Much of this will no doubt reflect an increase in demand in China and India where investors are not necessarily able to easily access the more transparent route of ETFs. High prices will be dependent on this latent demand exceeding the fundamental surplus. This scenario could conceivably be broken as this fundamental surplus is fed as a result of high prices encouraging more mine project development and leading to the loss of fabrication demand.

chunk.text: s this fundamental surplus is fed as a result of high prices encouraging more mine project development and leading to the loss of fabrication demand. However, project funding is far from forthcoming and there is only limited price elasticity of demand. Much of investors’ interest in silver stems from their need for a safe haven and, despite efforts to restore confidence in the global economy, they still believe that there is a high degree of uncertainty. US confirmation of a third round of quantitative easing pushed all metal prices higher but gold and silver have rallied by most with the gold/silver price ratio actually slipping back towards 50. We believe that investor demand for silver will be sustained for so long as interest rates remain low. Three months ago, US interest rates were forecast to rise from 0.4% in 2014 to 1.7% in 2015 and to 3.7% in 2016. They are now forecast to increase to only 0.8% in 2015 and to just 1.6% in 2016. We therefore now have silver’s price holding up through 2015.

2016. They are now forecast to increase to only 0.8% in 2015 and to just 1.6% in 2016. We therefore now have silver’s price holding up through 2015. Our forecast assumes that silver will hold up throughout 2013-14, rising and falling with the ebb and flow of short-term confidence in the global economy and averaging $31/oz in today’s US dollar terms. However, as more robust confidence is restored and monetary policy begins to tighten, we forecast silver’s price falling sharply by the end of 2015. We expect this eventual withdrawal by investors to undermine silver more swiftly than gold. The gold/silver price ratio has previously increased swiftly from 50 to 80-90 and we have such a swing and a decline in gold’s price pushing silver down towards $15/oz in 2016-17. However, these stemmed from low Australian dollar-denominated zinc prices and the silver output lost was not significant. Even with its brief fall to $27-28/oz in May-August, silver was still strong enough to encourage new mine development. Silver mining companies have continued to move their existing projects forward towards a full development decision as well adding to their project pipelines as former prospects appear viable with little or no work. Meanwhile, it also appears that the universe of zinc/lead mines in China has evolved a little.

relations.evidence: forecast mine production
Silver mining companies have continued to move their existing projects forward towards a full development decision

chunk.text: former prospects appear viable with little or no work. Meanwhile, it also appears that the universe of zinc/lead mines in China has evolved a little. Production has not fallen as a result of earlier low zinc and lead prices. Indeed, although we know that the official production figures are overstated, we still believe that Chinese output has been growing strongly. Given that silver’s price is still attractive to developers, we have our short-term forecast of mine supply being largely constrained by the speed with which companies can bring their projects on stream. We have global mine supply increasing by 1,750 tonnes in 2012-14, and this is largely attributable to committed developments that are fully financed and permitted. Of course, potential output is much higher in 2015 as a result of Pascua-Lama boosting committed production and a swathe of less certain ventures being theoretically in a position to supply at that point. However, project timelines will no doubt be stretched. Moreover, the fall in silver’s price from 2015 will result in global mine production becoming increasingly encumbered.

relations.evidence: mine supply being largely constrained by the speed with which companies can bring their projects on stream

chunk.text: es will no doubt be stretched. Moreover, the fall in silver’s price from 2015 will result in global mine production becoming increasingly encumbered. A combination of the price-induced curtailments at existing mines and of new projects, as well as a wave of likely exhaustions, will lead to global mine production peaking in 2015 at 27,900 tonnes and falling by 1,500 tonnes in 2016-17. At 26,400 tonnes in 2017, it will be 3,000 tonnes less than its full potential and 3,325 tonnes below its potential peak in 2016. chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 46 5.4 Supply-demand balance and stock changes Our tabulations show that the fundamental surplus in the silver market swelled to about 5,750 tonnes in 2011 from less than 4,200 tonnes in 2010. This increase of 1,550 tonnes stemmed from fabrication demand slipping by 1.2% (or 320 tonnes), while mine production rose by 1.6% (or 380 tonnes) and recycled scrap supply jumped by 12.4% (or 875 tonnes). We have the net supply from derivatives halving to 750 tonnes and we therefore estimate that the market’s overall surplus expanded from less than 5,700 tonnes to a little over 6,500 tonnes. The existence of silver’s market surplus has appeared to have been clearly identifiable in previous years because of the rapid growth in the holdings that are used to back exchange- traded funds (ETFs). However, even in 2009-10, our tabulations were beginning to imply that other unreported purchases were being made. The increase in ETF holdings net of official sector transactions and exchange movements did not entirely account for our estimate of the market’s overall surplus in those years.

relations.evidence: mine production rose by 1.6% (or 380 tonnes)
recycled scrap supply jumped by 12.4% (or 875 tonnes)

chunk.text: net of official sector transactions and exchange movements did not entirely account for our estimate of the market’s overall surplus in those years. Unreported holdings are therefore estimated to have increased by about 3,500 tonnes, and our numbers for 2011 suggest that, in contrast to the fall in ETF holdings, unreported purchases totalled about 7,700 tonnes. The market’s fundamental balance has not changed much in 2012. We have mine output rising by 2.3% (or about 575 tonnes) but recycled scrap supply falling by 12% (or 925 tonnes). Overall supply is therefore set to have fallen by 1.4% (or about 450 tonnes). However, we have fabrication demand slipping as well. We have it decreasing by 0.7% (or about 200 tonnes). The market’s fundamental surplus has therefore shrunk by only around 250 tonnes to 5,500 tonnes in 2012. The overall surplus will be dependent on how net supply from derivatives evolves. chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Silver 47 Known stock changes so far in 2012 suggest that much of this year’s surplus of 5,500 tonnes is being purchased by unreporting entities. However, that is not to say that reported holdings fell overall as they did in 2011. Total exchange stocks, principally on Comex, have increased by 725-750 tonnes, while total ETF holdings have lifted by 750-775 tonnes to their highest level since their peak in early 2011. If this trend was to continue for the balance of the year, we could conclude that unreported purchases will amount to 3,500 tonnes in 2012. We estimate that total stocks of all kinds will have lifted to the equivalent of two years’ fabrication demand at the end of 2012. This means that the stock-to-consumption ratio has doubled since 2006 and increased by half a year in the two years since the end of 2010. Our tabulations also show that unreported stocks are increasing during 2012 to a level that is just in excess of ETF holdings. We do not foresee the market’s surplus shrinking significantly in the short term, with a fundamental excess running at approximately 5,000 t/y in 2013-15.

relations.evidence: Known stock changes so far in 2012 suggest that much of this year’s surplus of 5,500 tonnes is being purchased by unreporting entities.

chunk.text: not foresee the market’s surplus shrinking significantly in the short term, with a fundamental excess running at approximately 5,000 t/y in 2013-15. Fabrication demand is projected to increase at 3-3.5% per annum during these three years and therefore offtake is rising by an absolute tonnage of about 900 tonnes each year. Meanwhile, we forecast mine supply increasing by an average of about 950 tonnes in each of those three years. Overall supply will not be up by quite this much because recycled scrap supply is expected to fall. Nevertheless, even though fabrication is forecast to rise by more than supply, excess metal will still be generated because demand and supply are starting from a point in 2012 where the latter is already about 5,500 tonnes in excess of the former. Even though we are projecting total stocks rising by approximately 15,000 tonnes between the end of 2012 and the end of 2015, the stock-to-consumption ratio will increase from 2 years to 2.3 years because fabrication demand is forecast to be rising at an arguably healthy rate of 3- 3.5% per annum.

relations.evidence: not foresee the market’s surplus shrinking significantly in the short term, with a fundamental excess running at approximately 5,000 t/y in 2013-15.

chunk.text: n ratio will increase from 2 years to 2.3 years because fabrication demand is forecast to be rising at an arguably healthy rate of 3- 3.5% per annum. Developments beyond 2015 are very much dependent on our assumption that silver’s price will be much lower at the end of 2015 compared with what it was at the start of 2015. The global economy is expected to be sufficiently strong at this point for monetary policy to begin being tightened. Interest rates will begin rising and investors will no longer be seeking to buy and hold silver as a form of safe haven. Although 2016-17 is expected to be characterised as a period of economic strength, we have growth in fabrication demand moderating compared with previous years. Industrial demand should certainly be performing well, and improved consumer confidence and lower silver prices should give a boost to demand for jewellery and silverware. However, we doubt that demand for silver coinage will be maintained at current levels. The Global Financial Crisis and ensuing period of economic uncertainty led to coinage production increasing by over 2,000 t/y.

relations.evidence: Global Financial Crisis and ensuing period of economic uncertainty led to coinage production increasing by over 2,000 t/y.

chunk.text: ned at current levels. The Global Financial Crisis and ensuing period of economic uncertainty led to coinage production increasing by over 2,000 t/y. While we do not have this demand for silver coinage returning to pre-crisis levels, we do assume that annual offtake in this sector will fall by around 1,500 t/y. This will limit overall fabrication demand so that growth slows to an average of less than 1% per annum in 2016 and 2017. Meanwhile, our forecast fall in silver’s price will have major repercussions for mine supply. We expect prices of around $15/oz to stifle project development with many potential new mines failing to be commissioned. With supply from today’s committed operations also contracting by then as a number of important exhaustions more than offset gains elsewhere, we forecast total annual supply falling by 1,200 tonnes in 2016-17 during which time offtake will lift by 550 tonnes. The market’s surplus in 2017 will therefore have shrunk towards 3,000 tonnes and the stock-to-consumption ratio will by then be stabilising at little more than 2.5 years.

relations.evidence: coinage production increasing by over 2,000 t/y

Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 CRU International Limited confidential Chapter 2 Gold Gold takes off as major central banks embark on additional monetary easing The gold market was repeatedly building in price premiums this year on expectations that the Federal Reserve would launch a QE3 or some other form of monetary policy easing. These expectations were based in part on weaker-than-expected economic data, in particular weak employment data. The Federal Reserve’s Chairman, Mr. Ben Bernanke, however, was taking his time and investors sold off their gold in disappointment every time they had been unable to find any clue to future easing intentions in his regular speeches after meetings of the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC). chunk.text: e laid in late July when Mario Draghi’s bombastic “whatever it takes” statement of support for the euro depressed the US dollar and sent gold higher. In mid-August, as the effects from the ECB’s strong rhetoric began to fade due to the lack of concrete steps gold received a fresh upside impetus from its fellow precious metal, platinum. Strong gains in platinum prices which surged on the back of labour unrest in South Africa dragged gold higher due to the possible threat of strikes spreading to the gold producers. Importantly, shortly before the US Federal Reserve decided to announce a third round of quantitative easing, the European Central Bank had finally moved from words to actions and promised to unroll an “unlimited bond buying” programme to lower the borrowing costs of troubled eurozone countries. Such a move also contributed to the gold rally by boosting inflationary expectations in Europe. As a result, gold hit fresh record highs above €1,360/oz. Furthermore, shortly after the QE3 announcement, the Bank of Japan became the third major central bank in just three weeks to launch a massive economic stimulus programme, lending further support to gold.

Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Gold 61 CRU International Limited confidential Central banks and investors continue to accumulate gold Whilst major central banks have been warming up their “printing presses”, their colleagues, predominantly in emerging markets, have been increasing their gold holdings to diversify away from the USD and the EUR and as an overall strategy of portfolio diversification. According to the IMF, net official sector purchases of gold amounted to slightly over 106 tonnes during the first six months of 2012. Although positive, this volume is 42% lower than a year earlier. Central banks of the Philippines and Russia were the most active buyers during the period. Their respective purchases totalled 35.3 tonnes and 35.1 tonnes. Smaller amounts were purchased by Kazakhstan (19.5 tonnes), Mexico (19.3), Belarus (5 tonnes) and Ukraine (4.8 tonnes). Fairly speaking, the 48.9 tonnes increase in gold holdings of the Turkish central bank over the same period, as reported by the IMF, was by far the largest half-year increment in absolute terms.

relations.evidence: According to the IMF, net official sector purchases of gold amounted to slightly over 106 tonnes during the first six months of 2012.

chunk.text: as little different in rhetoric from what the market had heard many times before; Mr. Bernanke again commented that he would hold fire on further QE. Following the established pattern, gold was sold off, testing support at $1,600/oz on August 1st. The market sentiment started to change after the minutes from that FOMC meeting were published on August 22nd. Unexpectedly, the document revealed a noticeably dovish tone of the committee for additional monetary accommodation. As investors were rapidly re-evaluating the prospect of QE3 the gold price broke through $1,650/oz the next day for the first time since early May. Mr. Bernanke’s post Jackson Hole comments helped to strengthen market confidence further and drove gold sharply higher to $1,692/oz on September 3rd on the widespread belief that they hinted at further quantitative easing. A few days later, gold broke through $1,700/oz and rallied to $1,737/oz as the market had become increasingly convinced in the inevitability of QE3 after publication of worse-than expected US employment data on September 7th.

chunk.text: he market had become increasingly convinced in the inevitability of QE3 after publication of worse-than expected US employment data on September 7th. Less than a week later, investors’ patience eventually paid off as the Federal Reserve announced a raft of monetary easing measures in a bid to help America’s weakening economy. Inflation expectations stoked by the launch of the new stimulus measures in the USA catapulted gold to $1,784.5/oz on September 21st, a new year-to-date high. It has since been consolidating in a $1,755/oz to $1,785/oz range. Overall, between the start of this year and September 24th, the gold price rose by over 10% in US dollar terms which ranks it third in terms of performance among the major four precious metals. Admittedly, the expectation of QE3 was not the only driving force behind the recent rally in gold. Its foundations were laid in late July when Mario Draghi’s bombastic “whatever it takes” statement of support for the euro depressed the US dollar and sent gold higher.

chunk.text: gold holdings of the Turkish central bank over the same period, as reported by the IMF, was by far the largest half-year increment in absolute terms. However, these additions to this bank’s balance sheet are a result of a new policy which allows commercial banks to pledge gold as part of their reserve requirements to the central bank. Since this gold is a form of bank deposit, rather than acquisition by the central bank in the open market, we do not include it in our data. Details of central bank buying interest continued to circulate around the market during July and August. For instance, South Korea’s central bank announced in August that it had bought 16 tonnes of gold in July. We will report on these changes in our next issue after they have been reflected in official statistics releases. Meanwhile, cumulative sales of only 5.1 tonnes of gold were reported by central banks under the third year of Third Central Bank Gold Agreement which runs through the end of September. Although there has been speculation that some eurozone banks might be driven to sell gold to help address the Euro situation, we see no sign of this happening.

chunk.text: here has been speculation that some eurozone banks might be driven to sell gold to help address the Euro situation, we see no sign of this happening. Bank of International Settlements was amongst the other few sellers of gold. In its annual report for fiscal 2011-12 the bank disclosed information on the sale of 3 tonnes of gold, ipso facto bringing down its total holdings to 116 tonnes. This compares with the sale of 1 tonne in fiscal 2010-11. Along with the official sector, retail and institutional investors have also continued to increase their exposure to gold through ETFs. After dropping to 2,251 tonnes at the end of July, from the then record high of 2,271 tonnes set in June, ETF positions then soared to a new record high of 2,310 tonnes in August on growing expectations of QE3. However, the growth did not stop there, and the post-FOMC buying frenzy propelled combined gold ETF holdings to another all-time high of around 2,350 tonnes by mid-September, an increase of nearly 108 tonnes for the year. Buying was seen in most of the funds which we monitor and sentiment seems to be becoming increasingly positive for gold despite the evidence of otherwise lacklustre physical demand.

chunk.text: 62 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Gold CRU International Limited confidential Gold ETFs are still seen as an attractive form of investment -30 -20 -10 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 tonnes  Fundamental surplus remains a feature of gold market Weak physical demand, notably in emerging nations, has been a significant drag on the gold market this year. According to the World Gold Council, demand for gold jewellery in 2012 H1 was 13% lower than for the same period in 2011. The main reason for the decline was lacklustre demand in India, the world’s largest gold consumer. Record high domestic prices due to the weak rupee and a poor monsoon season had deterred purchases of this metal. In addition, increased import duties had further aggravated the situation and domestic demand in 2012 H1 fell by 24.5% year-on-year to a total of 277 tonnes. This is slightly higher than we quoted in June.

chunk.text: ted the situation and domestic demand in 2012 H1 fell by 24.5% year-on-year to a total of 277 tonnes. This is slightly higher than we quoted in June. Taking into account reports of increased smuggling of gold into the country and our expectations of stronger jewellery sales in India during the second half of the year due to the Diwali festival, a key traditional gold buying occasion in the Hindu calendar, we have raised our 2012 forecast from 490 tonnes to 500 tonnes. Nevertheless, growth prospects for Indian gold jewellery demand have been somewhat muddled by the comments from the Deputy Governor of the Reserve Bank of India which made the RBI’s intentions concerning gold rather clear. “There is no intrinsic value in the speculative investment of gold... The precious metal cannot be used for anything productive.” He then called for a “social and cultural revolution” especially on the religious front to reduce demand for gold which will, in turn, ease the current account situation.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Gold 63 CRU International Limited confidential Meanwhile, gold jewellery demand in China increased by almost 1% to 250 tonnes in 2012 H1 from year earlier levels. The growth, however, was primarily concentrated in Q1 when the Chinese New Year festivities helped to drive consumption higher. Demand in the typically weaker Q2 was 40% lower than in Q1 as the slowing of GDP growth and the lack of a clear trend in the gold price discouraged Chinese consumers from purchasing gold. However, the situation may improve in the second half of the year on the back of the resumption of economic growth following the monetary easing implemented during the summer. Also, Chinese consumers are known for their preference of buying into a rising gold market. The fact that gold has been on an uptrend since early August may therefore stimulate additional purchases. As such, we maintain our forecast for the 2012 Chinese gold jewellery demand at 560 tonnes. Overall, we now expect 2012 global gold jewellery consumption to decline by slightly less than 5% year-on-year to a total of 1,868 tonnes.

relations.evidence: gold jewellery demand in China increased by almost 1% to 250 tonnes in 2012 H1 from year earlier levels.

chunk.text: 60 tonnes. Overall, we now expect 2012 global gold jewellery consumption to decline by slightly less than 5% year-on-year to a total of 1,868 tonnes. We have now extended our forecast to 2017 by when demand will have likely recovered to 2,271 tonnes, led by lower gold prices and increasing affluence of the population in emerging markets. In the first half of 2012, demand from various industrial applications of gold slipped by 5.5% year-on-year to 221 tonnes. The key electronics sector registered a 4.5% year-on-year decline to 157 tonnes as a consequence of the weakening global economy. This is much as we expected in June, and we have therefore left our projections for industrial demand unchanged. As we mentioned earlier, central banks continue to diversify their foreign exchange reserves into gold. The lack of counterparty risk associated with owning gold makes it especially attractive for central banks trying to reduce their exposure to the USD and the EUR.

relations.evidence: gold jewellery consumption
industrial demand for gold

chunk.text: ounterparty risk associated with owning gold makes it especially attractive for central banks trying to reduce their exposure to the USD and the EUR. However, year-on-year absolute net growth in the banks’ gold reserves slowed down significantly from almost 184 tonnes purchased in 2011 H1 to only 106 tonnes bought in the first half of 2012 suggesting that gold demand from this source has started to weaken. To reflect this, we have cut our forecast of net official purchases this year from 450 tonnes to approximately 300 tonnes. In the years ahead, buying activity is expected to continue to ease with only 25 tonnes of gold purchased on a net basis in 2017. The world’s gold supplies were largely unchanged in the first half of 2012 as a marginal rise in primary gold supply was largely offset by the fall in supply generated by gold recycling programmes. Recycling activity was down across virtually all markets as the gold price in US dollar terms remained relatively weak through the period. The Indian gold market was one of a few exceptions but the increase in the amount of scrap generated by this local gold market was not sufficient to offset weakness elsewhere.

relations.evidence: supply generated by gold recycling programmes

Remarkably, the country’s current account deficit for fiscal year 2011-12 was the highest in 30 years at 4.2% of GDP, primarily driven by expanding oil and gold imports, according to the Indian government.

chunk.text: one of a few exceptions but the increase in the amount of scrap generated by this local gold market was not sufficient to offset weakness elsewhere. However, we think that the recent surge in the US dollar price of gold together with the rupee-denominated price staying near all-time highs should have a positive effect on secondary supply during the second half of this year and result in year-on-year growth, albeit of a smaller magnitude than was expected in June. We have therefore cut our projection for gold scrap supply in 2012 to 1,700 tonnes from our previous figure of 1,750 tonnes. To summarise, the gold market is expected to remain in fundamental surplus over the next five years. As a result of our latest revision, gold’s fundamental surplus for this year is expected to amount to 1,468 tonnes, which is 6% higher than our June projection. After peaking at 1,533 tonnes in 2013, the surplus may then decline during the 2014-17 period and eventually shrink to

chunk.text: 64 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Gold CRU International Limited confidential 1,112 tonnes, driven by slowing mine production, tighter scrap supplies, and an improvement in demand from the jewellery and electronics sectors stemming from lower prices and rising incomes in many key developing countries. Gold’s response to QE may not last long With the central banks in both the USA and Europe now engaging in an unprecedented degree of monetary easing, it is no surprise that gold has rallied. Indeed, massive liquidity interventions, not to mention the “open ended” or “unlimited bond buying” nature of the latest stimulus initiatives announced by both banks, stoke investors’ fears of hyperinflation which will in turn encourage them to flock into hard assets, with gold being one of the most obvious options. However, our analysis suggests that the current gold rally has been primarily driven by the wave of “hot money” rushing into this market rather than by solid fundamentals. As such, we expect the energy that QE3 contributed to the gold rally to dissipate in a few months as the threat of real inflation has yet to materialise.

chunk.text: on in check the Federal Reserve began developing an exit strategy well before the large-scale asset purchase programmes (QEs) were fully implemented. So far, the strategy has been highly efficient; inflation in the US remains near a 30-year low even after a combined total of $1.8 trillion have been injected into the US economy during QE1 and QE2. Ten-year expected inflation and inflation risk premium  In our view, this same strategy is likely to keep a lid on inflation during QE3 too. To understand how and why it works, and what ramifications it may have for gold, we need to get deeper into the mechanics of QE. When the Federal Reserve engages in QE, it buys government securities or mortgage-backed securities (MBS) from banks by crediting their accounts at the Federal Reserve. As a result of these purchases, the levels of securities on banks’ balance sheets decline and their levels of reserves rise. Banks are required to hold a percentage of their customers’ transaction accounts as reserves at the Federal Reserve, but reserve balances greater than those required are considered to be excess reserves.

In contrast, the US Federal Reserve did not introduce any new “sterilisation” measures when it was embarking on QE3. But neither had it done so when it was launching QE1 and QE2. So on the surface it may seem like the Federal Reserve has been pumping extra money directly into the economy. However, if those newly created dollars did find their way into the US economy during the first two rounds of quantitative easing, housing and wages (the largest drivers of inflation) would have shot up a long time ago. But they have remained low, and overall price increases have remained within the Federal Reserve’s 2% per annum target range. To keep inflation in check the Federal Reserve began developing an exit strategy well before the large-scale asset purchase programmes (QEs) were fully implemented.

There are two factors which have been keeping lending constrained and prevented inflation from spiralling. In October 2008, the Federal Reserve received permission from the US Congress to pay interest of 25 basis points on excess reserves. Before that time, banks sought to minimise their holdings of excess reserves by making interbank loans in the federal funds market.

However, the effective federal funds rate has been consistently below 25 basis points since December 2008 which is of little surprise since the Federal Reserve can affect the federal funds rate by trading government securities. Because the current rate paid on excess reserves exceeds the rate a bank would receive in the federal funds market, it prefers to hold reserves in the Federal Reserve instead of lending them to other banks. In our view, banks are likely to continue accumulating excess reserves as long as the interest the Federal Reserve pays on excess reserves remains higher than the effective federal funds rate. The Federal Reserve’s latest announcement that it would keep the federal funds rate at rock- bottom rates at least through mid-2015 should further diminish the incentive for banks to lend.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Gold 67 CRU International Limited confidential The federal funds rate fell below the level of interest on excess reserves shortly after its introduction in late 2008, % 0.00 0.10 0.20 0.30 0.40 0.50 0.60 0.70 Interest rate on excessreserves  To sum up, QE is essentially an asset swap. When it is implemented, the banks’ balance sheets remain unchanged. The changes apply only to the portfolio composition of their financial assets together with a term structure. As such, the amount of money in circulation remains the same as before the launch of QE. As long as the Federal Reserve continues to encourage banks to maintain excessive reserves on its accounts whilst it cleanses their balance sheets of bad loans it is unlikely that a significant portion of this money will find its way into the real economy. Whether such strategy will eventually lead to economic growth remains to be seen. But what quantitative easing has been really successful at is improving market sentiment. Indeed, after every QE announcement investors think that the money supply will inflate and provide them with more money to invest.

90 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum CRU International Limited confidential Before last month's tragic news, the metal was confined to a tight trading range between $1,400/oz and $1,500/oz. At the end of June, a proposal that the euro zone’s bailout funds should be allowed to boost the capital of struggling commercial banks directly firmed the euro and pushed precious metal prices higher. Platinum too followed the suit as its price leapt from below $1,400/oz on June 28th to a high of $1,485/oz on July 4th. However, initial excitement quickly gave way to disappointment when the market decided to interpret the news about further QE by the UK, a rate cut by the People’s Bank of China and an interest rate cut by the ECB as demonstrations of how poorly those economies actually were rather than as positives for asset prices. Disappointing US jobs data and a fall in Chinese inflation on the back of waning demand added to the gloom and helped the US dollar to strengthen against the euro therefore depressing dollar-denominated commodity prices.

dominant National Union of Mineworkers (NUM) and aggressively rising to power Association of Mineworkers and Construction Union (AMCU), intensified. At some point during negotiations with the striking miners, the South African police lost control over the situation and opened fire killing a large number of strikers. When the news hit the wires, platinum immediately surged by $40/oz or nearly 3% to $1,440/oz as the market concluded that the death of so many miners might instigate similar labour actions at other mines and paralyze platinum output in the country. The platinum price continued to firm through the second half of August as the strike action at Lonmin continued and smaller but brief walkouts occurred at a few other mines in the Rustenburg area, putting further upward pressure on prices. As a result, platinum sustained its rally well beyond $1,500/oz. At the end of the month, however, some profit-taking set in

News on QE3 then came to the rescue, preventing platinum from seriously challenging this level. An outbreak of bullishness followed Mr. Bernanke’s post-Jackson Hole comments and platinum soared to end the month at $1,540/oz. The launch of QE3 and worsening of tensions in the South African platinum mining industry drove the price to $1,700/oz in mid-September before the upwards impetus dissipated. Investment activity in platinum ETFs effectively came to a standstill during the first two summer months; the net inflow of approximately 4 koz in June was largely offset by the net outflow of a similar magnitude in July. On the one hand, the lack of selling appetite was encouraging and suggested that some investors were still hoping to see higher platinum prices in the short to medium term and therefore maintained their positions in the ETFs.

of waning demand added to the gloom and helped the US dollar to strengthen against the euro therefore depressing dollar-denominated commodity prices. Against such macroeconomic background and persistent failure to reach a psychologically important price level of $1,500/oz, platinum weakened and eventually slid back towards $1,390/oz on July 23rd. The announcement on July 26th by the European Central Bank president Mario Draghi to “do whatever it takes to preserve the euro” prompted investors to close down short positions in the euro and some commodities, including that of platinum, which drove its price back above the $1,400/oz mark and all the way through to $1,427/oz on the final day of July. It did not stay there for long, though, as positive sentiment quickly disappeared after the Chairman of the Federal Reserve System, Mr. Ben Bernanke, announced no changes in its monetary policy on August 1st. Such decision disappointed the market and sent platinum plunging to $1,390/oz again on August 3rd.

chunk.text: d no changes in its monetary policy on August 1st. Such decision disappointed the market and sent platinum plunging to $1,390/oz again on August 3rd. The slide was arrested to an extent by bargain-hunting investors but the summer doldrums prevented the price from rising significantly higher and platinum continued to oscillate around the $1,400/oz level through to mid-August. The sluggishness of the platinum market during that period is best illustrated by the fact that even outbreaks of labour- related violence in early August, which led to a number of deaths at two mines in South Africa, namely Aquarius Platinum’s Kroondal and the Marikana platinum mine operated by Lonmin, initially failed to generate enough momentum for platinum to break out of a tight range around $1,400/oz. A much stronger shock that shortly followed eventually forced platinum prices to spring back into action though. On August 16th, the violence at Marikana, which stems from the rivalry between two competing labour unions the dominant National Union of Mineworkers (NUM) and aggressively rising to power Association of Mineworkers and Construction Union (AMCU), intensified.
