chunk.text: that some investors were still hoping to see higher platinum prices in the short to medium term and therefore maintained their positions in the ETFs. On the other hand, the lack of buying interest at those low price levels also indicated that a significant part of the investor community remained hesitant about future prospects for platinum based on concerns about a still weak industrial demand for this metal globally, and particularly in Europe. In addition, the continuous problem with the platinum market being in significant surplus acted as an effective dampener of any upward movement in the platinum price. In mid-August, however, as investors began to refocus on supply risks rather than shaky demand, combined holdings in platinum ETFs began to rise again. By the end of the month, the burst of buying, which constituted approximately 58 koz of platinum, took their overall holdings to a total of 1.38 Moz for the first time in five months.

92 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum CRU International Limited confidential Erosion of industrial demand for platinum continues Europe’s economic weakness continues to pose a great risk to platinum demand from local automakers as the implementation of austerity measures in some EU member states and low consumer confidence levels weigh on automotive demand in the region. According to LMC Automotive, August marked the 11th consecutive month of declines in regional car sales as they were 8.9% lower than in the same period of 2011. Overall, in the eight months of 2012, EU car sales were down by 7.1% year-on-year. The sales of commercial vehicles, which are equipped with bigger, predominantly diesel powered engines, and therefore use autocatalysts with a higher loading of platinum, fared even worse; their sales dropped by nearly 11% year-on-year in the first half of 2012, according to the European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA).

chunk.text: se; their sales dropped by nearly 11% year-on-year in the first half of 2012, according to the European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA). In spite of European car sales being on an uninterrupted downward trends for nearly a full year now, it is still hard to see any signs of improvement in European automotive demand in the remaining months of this year; the unabated euro-zone financial crisis and fiscal tightening witnessed across the region are likely to continue to hit consumer confidence and squeeze household budgets therefore curbing spending on big-ticket items such as cars. Additional deterioration in sales is now expected in Spain as this country increased its value-added sales tax by three percentage points on September 1st. Against this background, we expect automotive manufacturers to further reduce production in 2012 to minimise losses. Indeed, PSA Peugoet Citroen announced about its plan to shut down a production facility in Paris. This drop off in car production is expected to feed through to demand for platinum from the European automotive sector which we now expect to amount to 1,330 koz or 2% lower than our June estimate.

For these reasons, we envision demand for platinum rising to 770 koz only in 2017, or one year later than we expected in June. Despite the downgrade, we still consider the US carmakers of diesel vehicles to remain a reliable source of demand for platinum, at least over the next five years. Our view is supported by the plans of the various car manufacturers to introduce a number of additional diesel models in the USA over the next few years. These include: • BMW announced that the US market will see a 2.0-liter four cylinder diesel and 3.0-liter inline six diesel engine in the next 12 months.

As for car markets in other regions, sales of diesel cars continue to show an upward trend in the US car market as American consumers are increasingly looking for more fuel-efficient cars as a solution to rising prices for gasoline. According to a study released by hybridcars.com and Baum and Associates, sales increased by 23.4% over the first seven months of 2012 compared with the same time period in 2011. This is largely in line with our previous forecast and therefore we have left our 2012 outlook for platinum demand from US carmakers essentially unchanged. However, we have cut our projections for the next four years due to the potentially negative impact that the expiry of the payroll tax holiday might have on domestic demand for cars in 2013 and beyond. Furthermore, should the economic crisis in Europe deteriorate further, it would also have negative ramifications for the US economy as it would hurt consumer confidence and potentially curb demand for cars.

chunk.text: lems and huge disruptions to domestic manufacturing. With vehicle output and sales now returning to normal, demand for platinum is strengthening too. According to the Japan Automobile Manufacturers Association, automobile production in July 2012 was 16.7% higher than for the same month of 2011. Meanwhile, the Japan Automobile Dealers Association reported that vehicle sales rose by 37.5% in July. However, after surging by about 52% in the January-July period, domestic demand for cars seems to have gone ahead of itself; the double digit growth in sales over the last few months was to some extent caused by the subsidy programme for eco-cars introduced by the Japanese government in April of this year in a move aimed at boosting the ailing automobile industry. The programme, however, may run out of money as early as this month (only about 10% of the money allocated for car subsidies remained unspent as of August 27th, according to the trade ministry) and therefore the double digit growth in auto sales can be expected to drop at best to single digits in the coming months. Indeed, a contraction in sales cannot be completely ruled out. There are historical precedents for this.

relations.evidence: demand for platinum is strengthening too

chunk.text: . Meanwhile, the market share of standard-sized cars keeps falling due to a continued rise in the appeal of mini-sized vehicles for Japanese drivers. This, in turn, leads to lower precious metal content per car and lower platinum use overall. These developments mean that platinum demand from the Japanese automotive industry is likely to stay mostly flat, close to 500 koz per annum, over the next five years.

relations.evidence: lower platinum use overall

chunk.text: 94 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum CRU International Limited confidential On balance, given the increasingly difficult economic conditions in Europe and some other regions, we do not believe that auto production will live up to the expectations that we had at the beginning of the summer. That said, we still expect growth to continue throughout the remaining months of 2012, albeit at a slower pace. On this basis, we have lowered our forecast for platinum demand from the automotive industry for 2012 to 3,260 koz from 3,350 koz. We have also lowered our projections for the next five years and now expect a total of 19,980 koz of platinum to be consumed in the period between 2012 and 2017. As for our estimates for platinum demand emanating from other industrial applications, we are maintaining our forecast for platinum demand from the manufacturers of petroleum refining catalysts at 345 koz in 2012. Our outlook for the 2013-16 period remains largely unchanged. We also introduce the forecast for 2017, by when demand will have likely risen to 480 koz, led by growth in oil consumption in emerging Asian economies and recovering demand in the OECD economies.

Overall, between 2011 and 2017, platinum demand from this sector is set to rise by 22% to a total of 2,055 koz. We remain cautiously optimistic about the outlook for platinum demand in the electronics industry, as total PC shipments in 2012 are now expected to grow by less than 1% from last year, according to the research firm IDC. The obvious reason for such a fractional improvement in demand is the weak global economy which continues to curb consumer spending.

For example, car sales in January 2011, after the last programme ended, were down 21.5% on those of January 2010, when it was still in place. On this basis, we forecast only a slight rise, perhaps of no more than 2% year-on-year, in demand for platinum from local automakers in 2012. Of longer-term importance for national car demand, and therefore platinum too, is the fact that the Japanese parliament finally passed a long-debated social security and tax reform legislation in August. The key element was an agreement to double Japan’s consumption tax rate to 10% by 2015, after an interim increase to 8% in 2014, to tackle mounting national debt. The passage of this legislation prompts concerns that a new round of subsidies for car buyers is not likely to be introduced any time soon, leaving the domestic automobile industry to its own devices. Meanwhile, the market share of standard-sized cars keeps falling due to a continued rise in the appeal of mini-sized vehicles for Japanese drivers.

Another factor contributing to the deterioration of consumer demand for PCs is people’s increasing reliance on smartphones and tablet PCs, the devices equipped with solid state drives which, unlike HDDs, do not contain any mechanical parts and therefore do not require platinum coating. For these reasons, we have lowered our 2012 forecast of platinum demand from the electronics industry by 1.5% to 335 koz. We have also cut our projection for 2013, but still expect demand from this sector to peak out at the level of 345 koz in 2014. By 2017, which is now the final year of our outlook, consumption is expected to slide to 325 koz, or 1.5% below the 2011 level. As in our June outlook, we are maintaining our view that platinum demand from the glass industry is likely to experience a steep contraction in 2012 thanks to moderation of global production levels and still high level of excessive capacity in Asia. As such, platinum consumption is expected to fall by as much as 15% year-on-year to 1,525 koz.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum 95 CRU International Limited confidential Platinum miners begin to crumble under pressure from investors and labour unions Since our June update, many platinum mining companies have continued reporting disrupted mine production or shaft closures or delayed projects because of increased industrial action or poor economics. • Anglo American Platinum’s output of 1.03 Moz of refined platinum in the first half of 2012 was 13% lower than in the same period of last year. The drop in production was ascribed to the increased occurrence of safety-related work stoppages earlier in the year and to operational difficulties experienced at the re-start of its converter plant following a period of planned maintenance. This forced Amplats to cut its platinum production guidance for 2012 from 2.5-2.6 Moz to only 2.4-2.5 Moz (its original plan was to produce 2.7 Moz of platinum this year). The recent closure of Marikana will affect subsequent periods. More closures at Anglo’s less profitable mines could be anticipated later this year or in early 2013 following the completion of its operations review.

chunk.text: 98 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum CRU International Limited confidential • Wesizwe Platinum has begun blasting work at its flagship Bakubung mine in South Africa’s North West province. The main shaft will have a hoisting capacity of 230,000 tonnes, while the company expects to mine the Merensky reef ore at a rate of 180,000 t/m. Wesizwe expects to begin making deliveries in the second quarter of 2018 and will ramp up production to 350,000 oz/y by 2023. According to these reports, South Africa’s platinum industry was again in the epicentre of the latest wave of supply disruptions. Already struggling with soaring costs and weak global demand, the domestic platinum sector is now suffering from an intensifying union turf war. Over the past couple of years, the country has witnessed a number of extremely violent strikes and protests not only because of worsening poverty and low wages, but also because of the disputes between two rival unions, the established NUM and the AMCU which is aggressively recruiting new members.

Here is a timeline and brief overview of major strikes in the South African platinum industry in the last two years: • September - October 2010 A six-week long strike over pay at Northam Platinum’s Zondereinde mine resulted in a loss of around 40 koz of platinum. • May 2011 Lonmin dismissed 9,000 workers at its Karee operations, a part of the Marikana platinum mine, after they walked off in protest against the decision by the regional NUM office to suspend leadership of the Karee branch. Because of this industrial action, the company reported a loss of 30 koz platinum output. • October 2011 Aquarius Platinum’s Everest mine suffered a loss of approximately 5 koz due to a strike by certain employees of Murray and Roberts Cementation, a mining contractor used by the miner. The contract workers were represented by the AMCU. • January – February 2012 The closure of Impala Platinum’s Rustenburg operation for six weeks following clashes between the NUM and AMCU cost the company about 140 koz of platinum.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum 99 CRU International Limited confidential • July – August 2012 Unlawful strike action occurred at Kroondal’s Kwezi Shaft during July, accompanied by incidents of intimidation and violence as a result of the continuing tensions between rival mining unions. Lost production from the incident is estimated at 2,800 tonnes of a platinum containing ore. • August – September 2012 In the latest development, Lonmin was forced to halt production at all its South African operations, due to violent labour dispute between the NUM and AMCU members which engulfed its Marikana platinum mine in early August. A wildcat strike lasted for over a month and ended only after Lonmin had agreed to pay out a one-off back-to-work bonus worth 2,000 rand ($245) and grant an average salary increase of between 11% and 22% for its mineworkers. It is estimated that the company’s loss amounted to approximately 60 koz of platinum as a consequence of this confrontation. Despite the positive progress at Lonmin, the situation in South African platinum mining industry is far from over.

chunk.text: consequence of this confrontation. Despite the positive progress at Lonmin, the situation in South African platinum mining industry is far from over. In our view, a hefty wage increase the AMCU managed to negotiate with Lonmin has set up a new benchmark and may now trigger a rash of pay demands across the industry forcing other mining companies to play catch up as mineworkers demand equivalent or similar wage improvements to those agreed with Lonmin. It is illustrative that just within hours after Lonmin deal, mineworkers at neighbouring Anglo American Platinum operations demanded similar hikes and clashed with the police, suggesting more trouble is to come. That said, another increase in producers’ operating costs may jeopardize further platinum production in the country. Recent events appear to be a continuation of the dispute between the NUM and the AMCU which began to heat up in late 2011. The NUM is a dominant and a long established union in the South African mining industry with 300,000 members and official recognition at most mines.

chunk.text: 100 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum CRU International Limited confidential prestige during the next round of bi-annual wage negotiations. However, the risk of labour-related disruptions may be reduced if platinum producers in South Africa eventually decide to present themselves as a united front. Within the national precious metal mining industry, wage negotiations are carried out in two distinct styles. The Chamber of Mines, an industry body, negotiates with the two main unions (the NUM and Solidarity) on behalf of the main gold miners. In contrast, the platinum sector currently negotiates with unions on a company-by-company basis. This leaves individual mines open to labour discontent as rival organisations promise workers better deals. The fact that Impala Platinum has recently joined the Chamber of Mines as a full member and other majors are currently considering following its steps, suggests that the industry’s move towards collective bargaining is increasingly possible.

chunk.text: other majors are currently considering following its steps, suggests that the industry’s move towards collective bargaining is increasingly possible. Whether this helps to calm the things down in the short term remains to be seen, but overall we see it as a positive development in the longer run as labour unions tend to honour those agreements more. As for our outlook for 2012, we estimate that approximately 220 koz of South African platinum output has already been lost this year solely due to strikes. If we factor in safety stoppages, operational losses, and mine closures, then the supply picture takes on a more depressed look than we thought in June. As such, based on all of the recent production changes reported by platinum miners, we now expect global primary platinum production to dip this year by 11.6%, from 6,433 koz in 2011 to 5,689 koz. This is more than we thought in June when output was expected to drop by only 4.5% to 6,110 koz. The platinum market surplus is shrinking fast as troubles with supply deepen We have cut our June estimates for this year’s platinum mine supply and fabrication demand in order to reflect the challenges that platinum market continues to face.

relations.evidence: global primary platinum production to dip this year by 11.6%

chunk.text: The NUM is a dominant and a long established union in the South African mining industry with 300,000 members and official recognition at most mines. The union is affiliated with the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU), which is itself affiliated to South Africa’s governing political party, the African National Congress (ANC). In contrast, the AMCU, which was formed by a former NUM branch chairman, disgruntled with the NUM, is considered to be less political than the NUM and is not affiliated to COSATU. Before the Lonmin strike broke out it had only 30,000 or so members which made it larger than unions such as Solidarity (represents skilled workers) but much smaller than the NUM. However, by the end of August its membership doubled its size is growing rapidly thanks to the AMCU’s focus on workers’ issues including the questions of pay and grievances. With the NUM focusing on this year’s ANC elections which are taking place in December, it seems to be unable to respond quickly to worker concerns, so further gains by the AMCU can be expected in the future.

chunk.text: June estimates for this year’s platinum mine supply and fabrication demand in order to reflect the challenges that platinum market continues to face. We have lowered our platinum production forecast for this year by 744 koz or approximately 2.8% to 5,689 koz and cut our forecast for net fabrication demand by 197 koz or approximately 4% to 5,393 koz. Since the reduction in supply exceeds that in demand in absolute terms, the residual of 547 koz has been subtracted from our estimate of 2012’s surplus reducing the total figure to 296 koz from the 537 koz we forecast in June. This makes the size of this year’s surplus significantly (65%) lower than the 843 koz registered in 2011 (revised up from 807 koz in June). As the Eurozone remains in recession and world faces weak economic recovery, it seems increasingly unlikely that demand for platinum will be able to generate enough downward pressure to reduce the existing surplus. However, the much needed pressure may now come from the supply side of the market.

relations.evidence: June estimates for this year’s platinum mine supply and fabrication demand in order to reflect the challenges that platinum market continues to face.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum 101 CRU International Limited confidential price and have already resulted in significant re-rating. But the situation may reverse quickly should Lonmin strikers begin to return to work and mining companies manage to stop contagion from spreading to other South African operations. Because we already factored in a rise in the platinum price over the second half of this year in our June outlook, we just maintain our outlook for the platinum price to average $1,600/oz in 2012. In 2013, the decline in platinum mine production is expected to continue and reach 5.65 Moz based in part on the decisions made in 2012 to curtail or defer expansion plans. However, demand from global car manufacturers is expected to strengthen next year thanks to the introduction of Euro VI emissions rules for new vehicles coming into effect on January 1st 2013. As such, we think that double pressure from the both sides of the supply/demand equation can potentially reduce the market surplus even further in 2013 and bring the platinum market close to balance with the size of the surplus amounting to only 61 koz.

As a result, in the final year of our outlook, the platinum market should find itself in a surplus of approximately 67 koz. As for the platinum price, we expect it to have recovered to $1,730/oz in real terms or $1,957/oz in nominal terms by 2017.

112 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Platinum CRU International Limited confidential Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 CRU International Limited confidential Chapter 4 Palladium The supply-side issues affecting platinum lift palladium prices too After hitting a year-to-date high of $722/oz at the end of February palladium prices weakened notably. However, the escalation from a wildcat strike to fatal violence at Lonmin’s mine in South Africa prompted palladium to move up strongly higher in mid-August. The price rise through to mid-September accumulated to an impressive $123/oz or slightly more than 21%, with the metal’s quotations reaching $702/oz on September 14th. Admittedly, most of the rally was driven by the rise in platinum prices, although palladium also benefitted from more positive monetary actions from the US Fed and the ECB. But as the labour situation in South Africa began to normalise, palladium began to soften and was trading at $636/oz on September 24th. Despite a strong performance, palladium so far remains the worst performing major So far this year, palladium’s price performance has been uninspiring  550 600 650 700 750 US$/oz

chunk.text: 114 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Palladium CRU International Limited confidential precious metal; since the start of the year its price has fallen by 4%. Before recent rally, palladium was firmly stuck in a downward trend trying to find a support that would hold. However, its attempts to rebound were unsuccessful. After a steep correction in the second half of June, the palladium price rallied from $578/oz on June 28th to the $600/oz mark in the first few days of July. The rebound was driven by the broader risk rally sparked by the renewed hopes of QE3 as well as better than expected June results for car sales in the USA. The impetus, however, was not strong enough to breach the $600/oz level and the price fell back in the subsequent sell-off to record a new low for the year of $565/oz on July 23rd. Market sentiment subsequently recovered on various pro-euro comments by the ECB, which strengthened the euro and also provided an indirect impetus for the price of palladium lifting quotations to $591/oz at the end of the month. With little exciting macroeconomic news, palladium drifted for much of the first half of August.

constraints.condition: since the start of the year

chunk.text: fting quotations to $591/oz at the end of the month. With little exciting macroeconomic news, palladium drifted for much of the first half of August. However, a spike in labour unrest in the South African platinum on August 16th prompted the metal to move higher as investors concluded that the deficit that the palladium market is undoubtedly in this year may widen even further. As a result, palladium rallied from $576/oz on August 15th to $643/oz on August 24th before settling closer to $620/oz at the end of the month. During the months of June and July, palladium lost some of the investor favour that it had gained earlier in the year. Net outflows of approximately 61 koz of metal from global ETFs during this period nearly offset the strong buying seen in May. This selling corresponded with South Africa supply concerns are luring investors back in palladium ETFs  -60 -40 -20 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140 January February March April May June July August '000 oz

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Palladium 115 CRU International Limited confidential the fall in the palladium price below $600/oz and perhaps indicated growing dissatisfaction of some investors with poor price performance which had seen the metal falling by nearly 22% since this year’s peak of $722/oz in late February to a trough of $565/oz at the end of July. In August, price movements were more positive, however, and ETF net outflows finally gave way to net buying again as investors’ interest in palladium was slowly heating up by intensifying labour unrest in South Africa’s platinum belt. As a result, combined palladium ETF holdings increased by 19.5 koz in August. Overall, in the first eight months of the year, net demand amounted to 285 koz, suggesting that many market participants are still bullish on this precious metal. Underlying industrial demand for palladium remains healthy Palladium consumption continues to benefit from firm demand from the global automotive industry, particularly from automakers in the all-important US market. Overall, US auto sales have been strong this year, rising 15% through August, even as broader US economic growth has remained sluggish.

relations.evidence: Palladium consumption continues to benefit from firm demand from the global automotive industry, particularly from automakers in the all-important US market.

chunk.text: nt US market. Overall, US auto sales have been strong this year, rising 15% through August, even as broader US economic growth has remained sluggish. In fact in August, when car sales soared by 20% from a year ago, US consumers were buying new vehicles at the fastest pace since the beginning of the economic crisis. As in previous months, pent-up demand and easier credit terms were the major reasons behind this surge in sales. To reflect these positive developments, we have slightly raised our projections for palladium demand from this region and now expect local car manufacturers to consume a total of 1,688 koz this year, or 2% more than we thought in June. We have downgraded our outlook for the next four years, however, as the so-called “fiscal cliff” of automatic tax increases and government spending which the US economy may face at the start of 2013 can curb demand for cars, and therefore palladium, as consumers restrain spending. Meanwhile, in China, rapid economic growth gave way to more moderate rates in line with the government’s plans to stabilize the rapidly developing economy. This had a chilling effect on consumer demand this year, including automotive demand.

relations.evidence: We have slightly raised our projections for palladium demand from this region and now expect local car manufacturers to consume a total of 1,688 koz this year,

chunk.text: edit restrictions and other stimulus measures introduced by the Chinese government should start to boost demand for cars towards the end of the year. While we expect sales to accelerate in coming months, we predict vehicle output growth will somewhat slow in 2012 H2 as car dealers seek to drawdown inventories, cooling demand for palladium. In addition, some local governments are now less positive towards the automotive sector,

116 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Palladium CRU International Limited confidential seeing the environmental issues as outweighing the economic benefits. For example, the city of Guangzhou initiated restrictions on car sales at the start of July because of increasing congestion and pollution. When similar rules were imposed in Beijing new car sales fell from roughly 900,000 units in 2010 to 400,000 units last year. Other large cities such as Shenzhen and Hangzhou are expected to follow, limiting the potential growth rate of the Chinese car market and the potential for additional palladium demand in the long term. Elsewhere, the automotive market in Indonesia expanded with year-to-date production of vehicles increasing by 33.4% in July, whilst domestic sales also rose 25.9% over the same period. Meanwhile, the auto industry in Thailand is benefitting from the government’s “First Car Buyer” policy. Consumers are eligible for a tax reduction when they order a new first time car under this scheme. Indeed, auto sales rose 33.5% year-on-year in H1 2012.

relations.evidence: limiting the potential growth rate of the Chinese car market and the potential for additional palladium demand in the long term
year-to-date production of vehicles increasing by 33.4% in July, whilst domestic sales also rose 25.9% over the same period

chunk.text: umers are eligible for a tax reduction when they order a new first time car under this scheme. Indeed, auto sales rose 33.5% year-on-year in H1 2012. The introduction of eco-cars also helped support auto sales, as energy efficient cars help save costs on gas, as well as being environmentally friendly. Light vehicle sales in Russia increased by 39% last year and look set to rise by a further 15% in 2012 despite the end of a subsidy programme. Some car manufacturers see a very bright future for this market. Since all these are primarily petrol markets this growth is positive for palladium demand. To reflect recent changes in global automotive demand, we have raised our June estimate for this year’s palladium demand from the auto industry by 1% to 6,232 koz. We forecast 2012 combined demand for palladium from other industrial applications such as electrical, electronic, chemical and dental to total 3,675 koz. This is down by 15 koz from our previous forecast of 3,690 koz. Overall, after all the recent adjustments are taken into account and ignoring jewellery, gross industrial demand for palladium is now forecast to grow by 3% to a total of 9.9 Moz in 2012.

relations.evidence: growth is positive for palladium demand

chunk.text: government’s plans to stabilize the rapidly developing economy. This had a chilling effect on consumer demand this year, including automotive demand. The spectacular growth rates of 2010 or 2009 now may seem exorbitant, but the Chinese car market grew nonetheless in the first eight months of the year, albeit at a modest pace of 4.1%. To stimulate growth, the Chinese government plans to re-launch an RMB 6 billion stimulus plan to provide subsidies to rural residents who trade in old vehicles for new fuel-efficient ones. From the beginning of June, rural residents are eligible for handouts of up to RMB 3,000 for each locally made light vehicle with engine displacement of more than 1.6 litres. Light trucks with loading capacity of no more than 6 tonnes would also be eligible for government subsidies. At the same time, there are some worrying signs of excess capacity and rising inventory levels of unsold vehicles in China, but current expectations are that easing of credit restrictions and other stimulus measures introduced by the Chinese government should start to boost demand for cars towards the end of the year.

chunk.text: nts are taken into account and ignoring jewellery, gross industrial demand for palladium is now forecast to grow by 3% to a total of 9.9 Moz in 2012. This is in stark contrast with our projections for platinum demand which we expect to fall by 1.3% this year. Our forecast of a greater increase in demand for palladium versus platinum is based on more optimistic auto production forecasts for the USA, and moderate but still positive forecasts for China and other emerging-market regions. These regions are dominated by petrol- fuelled vehicles, which rely primarily on palladium-heavy autocatalysts and use proportionately far more palladium than platinum. Lower prices and labour strife in South Africa’s platinum belt weigh on palladium mine output The market sometimes forgets that where platinum is produced, so is palladium. As such any supply issues in South Africa can have a significant impact on the palladium market, since the country provides some 38% of the world’s supply of this metal.

relations.evidence: Our forecast of a greater increase in demand for palladium versus platinum is based on more optimistic auto production forecasts for the USA, and moderate but still positive forecasts for China and other emerging-market regions.

chunk.text: ssues in South Africa can have a significant impact on the palladium market, since the country provides some 38% of the world’s supply of this metal. Although only a few announcements over mothballing of active mining operations in this country have been made, existing production is being either heavily cut by the mining companies or increasingly often gets disrupted by labour unrest or safety-related closures. To reflect these developments our outlook for mine output from this country has undergone a major cut. We also forecast that North American production will ease somewhat this year as producers react to lower prices. • Norilsk Nickel produced 729 koz of palladium in 2012 Q2, representing a 12% growth as

relations.evidence: Our outlook for mine output from this country has undergone a major cut.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Palladium 117 CRU International Limited confidential compared to 2012 Q1. The increase in palladium output was largely driven by the growth of production at Russian divisions of the company. In the first half of 2012 the overall output of palladium totalled 1.38 Moz, 3% below the 1.43 Moz in the comparable period of last year. This was largely in line with the production plan. • North American Palladium reported that output of 40,017 oz of palladium from its Lac des Iles palladium mine in the second quarter was nearly 15% lower compared with the same period of last year. • Stillwater’s mines produced 102,800 oz of palladium during the second quarter of 2012, a 6.4% decrease from the 109,800 oz production in the second quarter of 2011 but a 9.4% increase from the 94,000 ounces produced during the first quarter of 2012. The company’s larger Stillwater mine recorded a 10% gain in production in the June period against a 4% rise at its East Boulder operation. o The company is also progressing with two development projects, Graham Creek and Blitz.

relations.evidence: output of palladium
from its Lac des Iles palladium mine in the second quarter

chunk.text: June period against a 4% rise at its East Boulder operation. o The company is also progressing with two development projects, Graham Creek and Blitz. Both can be accessed from existing mine infrastructure with completion planned for 2017-18, but with possible limited outturn from Blitz in three to four years. No production details on either project have yet become available. • Vale’s Canadian mines produced 66 koz of palladium in its second quarter to June, 8% below the comparable 2011 period of 72 koz, but up 12% from a poor first quarter. Taking into account the abovementioned changes in mine production, we expect global primary palladium supplies to decline this year by 7.5% to 6,345 koz, revised down from 6,643 koz previously. The fundamentals for palladium still point towards higher prices The poor price performance that palladium has been demonstrating so far in 2012 is somewhat confusing taking into account that underlying fundamentals seem rather attractive. Even despite slowing growth in the USA and most of the BRIC nations and a difficult economic climate in Europe, automotive demand is growing healthily in 2012.

relations.evidence: Vale’s Canadian mines produced 66 koz of palladium in its second quarter to June, 8% below the comparable 2011 period of 72 koz

constraints.condition: expected global primary palladium supplies to decline this year by 7.5% to 6,345 koz

chunk.text: te slowing growth in the USA and most of the BRIC nations and a difficult economic climate in Europe, automotive demand is growing healthily in 2012. Assuming that the world economy will eventually get back on track, the outlook for palladium demand is positive. Meanwhile, low platinum and nickel prices along with increasingly difficult labour relations in South Africa are placing pressure on primary producers of these two metals, often the same companies which are major producers of palladium. Because of this it is hard to see any growth in global palladium mine output this year. Given the cuts already announced at some South African platinum mines, the palladium market should move further into deficit, setting the scene for higher prices later in the year. Indeed, there appears to be strong support at close to the $570/oz level where palladium was hovering during July and early August. This was also close to the highs of April 2010 and early 2008 where significant resistance was found. If this support holds, then palladium at current prices looks fairly attractive.

118 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Palladium CRU International Limited confidential therefore lowering our average annual price forecast for 2012 to $670/oz even though we expect the market to move back into a deficit of approximately 132 koz in 2012, following a significant surplus of 1.78 Moz in 2011. In addition to purely fundamental drivers, our forecast of market deficit in 2012 (and beyond) is based on expectations of reduced Russian palladium stockpile sales. The widely accepted view is that these stocks could be exhausted by the end of 2013. Swiss import statistics for the January-August period showed that imports of palladium from Russia into Switzerland totalled nearly 68 koz. That said, no significant shipments were registered between April and August, reinforcing the market view that sales from this source may be drying up. For the years beyond 2012, we expect the palladium deficit to keep widening such that it gets close to 1.47 Moz in 2017. A swing back into persistent and widening deficit supports our view that prices are likely to be on an uptrend trajectory over the next few years and perhaps average $1,080/oz in real terms in 2017.

relations.evidence: prices are likely to be on an uptrend trajectory over the next few years and perhaps average $1,080/oz in real terms in 2017

chunk.text: 128 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Palladium CRU International Limited confidential Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 CRU International Limited confidential Appendix A Industrial and economic outlook 1. Exports squeezed as sentiment deteriorates The highly uncertain outlook for the world economy is instilling a sense of downward drift in confidence globally, negatively impacting real activity as investment and consumption decisions are postponed. Nowhere is this more evident than in international trade. Growth in export volumes has been running close to 3% year-on-year since the end of last year, only half its trend rate, but indicators for the current quarter point to a further deceleration. All countries are being impacted to a degree by slower growth in world trade but those that have had the greatest reliance on exports as the driver of growth clearly have the most to lose, which includes much of northern Europe and eastern Asia.

entities.name_en: investment and consumption decisions are postponed

relations.evidence: Nowhere is this more evident than in international trade.
Growth in export volumes has been running close to 3% year-on-year since the end of last year, only half its trend rate, but indicators for the current quarter point to a further deceleration.

constraints.condition: since the end of last year

chunk.text: had the greatest reliance on exports as the driver of growth clearly have the most to lose, which includes much of northern Europe and eastern Asia. Similarly, industry, the sector of the economy with the greatest dependence on trade, is seeing growth drop faster than that of the global economy as a whole and we estimate expansion of just 2.5% year-on-year this quarter compared to 4.8% in the same period a year ago. The developed economies are unlikely to build a stronger growth dynamic anytime soon due to their deep-seated debt problems. The global recovery we forecast is therefore reliant on policy easing in the developing economies stimulating investment and consumption. • China’s economic recovery is so far failing to gain traction due to the headwinds from overseas and from excess inventories. We see the setbacks as short-term in nature and continue to expect a policy-led recovery into 2013. • Brazil is also struggling currently but aggressive stimulus measures should deliver a robust rebound next year. Still high Indian inflation is constraining monetary policy but this year’s slowdown should help ease upwards price pressures, paving the way for a pick-up next year.

chunk.text: 130 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A CRU International Limited confidential • Recovery in the US remains shallow and is vulnerable to shifts in confidence arising from events overseas or from shifts in policy at home as a result of November’s elections. The Japanese economy also continues to expand, but at a diminishing rate; further growth is largely dependent on reconstruction spending. Over the past three to six months our forecast revisions for GDP growth this year have broadly cancelled one another out at the global level. The developed economies have so far performed a little better than forecast and the developing economies a little worse (with Brazil being an outlier to the downside). Our revisions for next year are all downwards as the slow start to the second half of this year and the preponderance of downside risks point to a relative subdued global recovery. World GDP is now forecast to expand by a below trend 2.7% in 2013.

chunk.text: 134 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A CRU International Limited confidential 2. Regional developments 2.1 The Americas 2.1.1 USA: Shallow recovery continues Whilst there are few signs of actual slippage in terms of the latest US data releases, growth is very modest and the recovery remains vulnerable to shifts in confidence regarding the outlook for the future. In fact, US consumers and businesses seem reluctant to commit to expanding their spending given the high level of uncertainty surrounding the global economy and potential shifts in policy at home that could occur as a result of the November elections. The advance estimate for GDP in 2012 Q2 reflected a sharp slowdown in consumer spending, which was a drag on overall growth last quarter. GDP expanded at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of just 1.5% in 2012 Q2, on track with the consensus view, but slower than the 2% growth posted in 2012 Q1. Consumer spending rose by 1.5% last quarter, versus 2.4% in 2012 Q1. Total private fixed investment grew more rapidly, at 8.5% versus 6.1% in 2012 Q1, and the gain in private inventories essentially reversed the decrease experienced during 2012 Q1.

relations.evidence: GDP expanded at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of just 1.5% in 2012 Q2, on track with the consensus view, but slower than the 2% growth posted in 2012 Q1. Consumer spending rose by 1.5% last quarter, versus 2.4% in 2012 Q1.
Total private fixed investment grew more rapidly, at 8.5% versus 6.1% in 2012 Q1.
the gain in private inventories essentially reversed the decrease experienced during 2012 Q1

chunk.text: grew more rapidly, at 8.5% versus 6.1% in 2012 Q1, and the gain in private inventories essentially reversed the decrease experienced during 2012 Q1. Finally, government spending fell by 1.4% in 2012 Q2, a smaller decline than the 3% contraction in spending that was posted in 2012 Q1. Since consumer spending is the largest component of US GDP, clear progress must be made in terms of employment and income growth before we can project stronger GDP results. Currently, employment prospects are generally improving. Surveys indicate that employers are willing to hire, while the declining trend in the weekly initial claims data points to fewer new layoffs. That said, the unemployment rate in July was 8.3%, which is essentially unchanged since January. Thus, the economy is keeping pace with new entrants to the labour force, but long-term unemployed workers are still unable to find jobs. Incomes are only slightly outpacing inflation. In 2012 Q2, real disposable personal incomes increased by 1.3% compared to 2011 Q2.

Since this is below consumer spending growth last quarter, it does not appear very likely that spending will accelerate into 2012 Q3, particular given the recent data on consumer credit which show that Americans appear less willing to run up credit card debt. Moreover, consumer sentiment, as reported by the University of Michigan, remains at low levels, though at least the preliminary August reading indicates that the decline experienced in July has been reversed. Continued contraction in the new orders component of the July ISM Manufacturing survey points to a lack of new momentum in overall output, but as is usually the case at a transition point in the economy, there are rather large discrepancies between sectors that are performing well, and sectors that are lagging. Metals overall are mixed. While the primary and fabricated metals sectors reported growth in July, the transportation and machinery sectors indicate that contraction is expected.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A 135 CRU International Limited confidential June rose by 1.3% month-on-month, after increasing by 1.5% in May. However, excluding aircraft and defence goods, the data were more reflective of the ISM results – after posting 2.3% month-on-month gain in May, orders declined by 1.7% in June. The bright spot in the US economic outlook continues to be residential construction, with the July data release indicating that the pace of recovery in the sector may increase. Although total starts last month were 746,000 units annualized, slightly below the June level of 754,000 units, this marked a clear gain over the 2012 Q2 average of 736,000 units. We are likely to see higher levels of starts in the next few months given that July permits jumped to 812,000 units annualized, the highest monthly level recorded since August 2008. While the multi-family sector continued to make gains, the single-family sector also shows improvement, and the 513,000 permits registered in July mark the highest level in this indicator since March 2010.

entities.name_ru: уровень разрешений на строительство в США

relations.evidence: The bright spot in the US economic outlook continues to be residential construction, with the July data release indicating that the pace of recovery in the sector may increase.

chunk.text: he single-family sector also shows improvement, and the 513,000 permits registered in July mark the highest level in this indicator since March 2010. From a regional perspective, there have been notable gains in permits in both the South and West, which contained some the areas hit hardest during the housing crisis. The Northeast continues to exhibit an improving trend, while activity in the Midwest is stable. In summary, US economy continues to grow very modestly, but that the more forward- looking indicators point to a potential lull in activity, during 2012 H2. As a result of the weaker level of economic activity during 2012 Q2, we have revised down our GDP growth rate slightly to 2.2% for both this year and 2.3%in 2013. Between 2014 and 2016, our expectation of GDP growth in the range of 2.5% to 3.0% per year is unchanged. Given that economic conditions have not yet deteriorated sufficiently, either in the US or elsewhere in the world, we hold to our view that the Fed will not initiate another round of quantitative easing (i.e. QE3) prior the November elections.

chunk.text: 136 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A CRU International Limited confidential 2.1.2 Brazil and South America: Brazil launches further stimulus Economic growth in Brazil has continued to decelerate affected by the difficult global conditions this year, in particular slow US growth and contraction in the Eurozone. Despite a raft of stimulus measures, growth in industry and the economy more broadly has been subdued. Manufacturing has been struggling for the last few quarters, undermined by a strong currency and a slowdown in the global economy. And, although industrial activity returned to growth in June, after three consecutive months of decline, the modest 0.2% month-on-month gain is hardly a sign that the sector is gaining momentum. Other leading indicators for the manufacturing industry, such as the HSBC manufacturing PMI suggest that the industrial sector continues to be weak. The July reading remained below the 50 no-change threshold at 48.7, with both the new orders and output components falling for the fourth consecutive month. The slowdown in Brazil’s economy this year moved the government to announce in mid- August a fresh stimulus and investment package.

chunk.text: onsecutive month. The slowdown in Brazil’s economy this year moved the government to announce in mid- August a fresh stimulus and investment package. The $66bn plan is aimed at enhancing long- term growth and international competitiveness by improving infrastructure and reducing business costs associated with electricity and labour. By focussing on the supply-side the plan aims to target issues which historically have limited growth, such as bottlenecks, and these measures should benefit industry more directly. In other measures to stimulate demand, the central bank has cut interest rates to a record low of 8% as inflation has eased to around 5%. Notwithstanding the recent stimulus plan and pickup in June, we forecast a contraction in industrial production of around 2% this year followed by a rebound to 4.7% growth in 2013. Weaker than expected economic indicators so far this year mean that we have cut GDP growth rates for this year and next by around 30 and 60bps respectively. We now forecast economic growth of 1.8% this year and 4% in 2013. In Argentina, industrial production fell in July by 2.1% year-on-year.

chunk.text: espectively. We now forecast economic growth of 1.8% this year and 4% in 2013. In Argentina, industrial production fell in July by 2.1% year-on-year. Although better than expected, the figure marks the fourth straight month of contraction, partly reflected by weak demand from Brazil for Argentine cars. Argentina’s economy is slowing mainly due to high inflation, poor global growth and government imposed trade restrictions on industry. As such, we expect industrial production to contract this year by 0.6% before recovering to grow by around 3.6% next year. Our outlook for economic growth is for a sharp fall to just over 2% this year and to remain relatively subdued in 2013 with growth of 2.9%. 2.2 Europe 2.2.1 Western Europe: financial tensions ease but not enough Bond markets in the Eurozone have eased in recent weeks following calming words from the European Central Bank (ECB) that they are now willing to “do what it takes” to save the Eurozone. Nevertheless, economic activity data coming out of the region have been weak overall, with the core-periphery divide becoming even more pronounced.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A 137 CRU International Limited confidential It seems that, on a practical level, the ECB will restart purchasing bonds on the secondary market (via its secondary market bond purchase programme), meanwhile lending rescue fund money to struggling countries to purchase their own bonds on the primary market. As a consequence of this announcement, Spanish and Italian government bond yields have eased (to below 7% for 10-year debt in the case of Spain), and especially for shorter-term debt (where the ECB is likely to focus its support). The ECB made clear, however, that to qualify for further bond purchases a country would have to apply for assistance from the Eurozone’s rescue funds and accept “strict and effective conditionality”. It is unclear whether Spain and Italy would be prepared to accept this. Nevertheless, we expect Spain’s application to come before October. The ECB have also tried to reassure investors that it will drop its demand for seniority over other bond investors, which should further help ease pressure on yields.

Finally, there have been indications that the ECB is also willing to introduce a cap on peripheral bond yields, to be decided when it meets in early September. It's not clear what maximum 'spread' the ECB would choose, but historically a gap of 500 basis points over German borrowing costs has been seen as unsustainable, so the ECB would presumably choose a smaller gap. Despite the recent improvement in the bond markets, economic activity data coming out of the region have been weak. The Eurozone economy contracted by 0.2% quarter-on-quarter in 2012 Q2, better than we expected due to upside surprises in Germany (+0.3%), France (flat) and the Netherlands (+0.2%). However, the relative strength of the core economies was more than offset by sharp falls in activity in the periphery. Italy and Spain contracted, by 0.7% and 0.4% respectively, while Portugal shrank by 1.2% and Greece contracted by 6.2% year-on-year.

chunk.text: 138 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A CRU International Limited confidential in GDP in excess of 1%. Furthermore, the manufacturing sector has been shrinking rapidly. In July the Eurozone manufacturing PMI fell to 44.0, its lowest level since June 2009. Overall, we have not adjusted our forecast this month as any upside surprises coming from the core have been countered by the beleaguered periphery. We forecast a contraction of 0.9% this year, and another fall of 0.1% in 2013. We expect GDP will not return to pre-crisis peak level (i.e. early 2008) until mid-2015 at the earliest – a total of 30 quarters. In comparison, the Eurozone region took a total of just eight quarters to recover following the recession of 1992-93, and highlights the depth of the current malaise. In Germany, the debt crisis continues to batter confidence and impact demand for its goods from the rest of the continent. Although growth in 2012 Q2 was better than expected, with positive impulses coming from both consumer spending and net foreign trade, forward looking indicators suggest a significant slowdown in the second half of the year.

chunk.text: s coming from both consumer spending and net foreign trade, forward looking indicators suggest a significant slowdown in the second half of the year. July’s manufacturing PMI fell to 43.0, and indicated that production has fallen for the fourth consecutive month. Painting a similarly bleak picture, core factory orders fell by 1.7% month-on-month in June, with softer demand from the USA and China playing a part. Furthermore, the ZEW survey of investor confidence fell from -19.6 in July to -25.5 in August, reflecting the weak investment climate as nervous companies delay projects (fixed investment fell 1% in 2012 Q1). Consumer confidence continues to be damaged by the uncertain climate, and retail sales have not grown since the beginning of the year. The poor business climate is likely to feed into rising unemployment as the year progresses, although it should start falling again toward the middle of 2013. Meanwhile, house prices have increased by a ground-breaking 5.7% since 2010 after nearly two decades of stagnation. However, this is unlikely to unleash a spending boom.

German regulators are watchful, and owner-occupation in the country stands at just 46%, so any rise in prices has a fainter “wealth effect” than in Britain, for example, where about two-thirds of homes are owner-occupied. Overall, we now forecast growth of 0.2% this year, and 0.7% in 2013 (0.4% and 0.9% respectively last month). In the UK, 2012 Q2 GDP posted a worse-than-expected contraction of 0.7% quarter-on- quarter, driven mostly by the extra bank holiday in June. Looking ahead, the debt overhang means that consumers will be unable to drive the recovery alone. Also, exports and business investment are constrained by the ongoing Eurozone crisis. This means we see slow progress in the recovery, and we forecast GDP falling by 0.4% this year, before recovering to grow by just 1.2% in 2013. 2.2.2 Russia and Eastern Europe: not immune to slowdown Russia is also not immune from the Eurozone crisis, and the general slowdown elsewhere.

chunk.text: 2013. 2.2.2 Russia and Eastern Europe: not immune to slowdown Russia is also not immune from the Eurozone crisis, and the general slowdown elsewhere. GDP grew at 4.0% year-on-year in the 2012 Q2, the weakest pace since the same quarter of 2011 and down from 4.9% in the January-March period. Recent data point to a two-speed economy. The Eurozone crisis and a slower China is sapping demand for commodities and hurting industry – the trade surplus in June was the smallest since November 2010. A strong ruble exchange rate versus the euro is also hitting competitiveness against European manufacturers.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A 139 CRU International Limited confidential On the other hand, accelerating wage growth, slowing unemployment and consumer optimism at the highest level in almost four years are driving private spending, which accounts for half of Russia’s economy. However, inflation is high (5.6% in June) due to a weaker harvest, and a rise in interbank rates is hurting lending growth. This will constrain consumption growth in the second half of 2012. Overall, we now forecast 3.8% growth this year, and 2.9% in 2013. Meanwhile, in Eastern and Central Europe, the situation is worse. We expect Hungary and the Czech Republic to be in recession until 2013 Q2, while Poland, Turkey, and the Baltic states should grow moderately. The recovery in Romania's economy after two years of severe recession is now coming under threat, victim to the months-long political crisis that has engulfed the country. Economic policy has been held hostage by the political war underway between the ruling Social-Liberal Union coalition and centre-right President Traian Basescu, targeted by an impeachment move launched six weeks ago.

We now only see growth of 0.3% this year, and 1.1% in 2013. 2.3 Asia 2.3.1 China: Struggling exports and excess inventories prevent recovery from gaining traction China’s economic recovery is failing to gain traction as the headwinds from weaker overseas markets and excess inventories offset the positives from the policy loosening that began at the end of last year, and which has been stepped up since the spring. Indeed, the latest indicators point to further downside risks to the recovery, with it increasingly appearing that 2012 Q3 will disappoint relative to expectations. chunk.text: 140 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A CRU International Limited confidential The external environment is acting as an increasing drag on the Chinese economy. Last month export growth slowed to just 1.0% year-on-year in nominal US$ terms from 11.3% in June. Shipments to the developed economies led the deceleration, with those to the US slowing from growth of 10.6% year-on-year to 0.6% and those to the EU contracting by 16%. After allowing for exchange rate and price effects, export volumes showed no growth compared to a year earlier. Import growth also slowed last month, but to a lesser extent than exports. The value of imports in US$ terms grew by 4.7% compared to 6.2% the prior month, while volume growth picked up to 8.8%. The trend in the growth rate of import volumes is upward and suggests that domestic demand is strengthening. Nevertheless, with the rate of increase of import volumes comfortably outpacing that of exports, net trade is reducing GDP growth. Household consumption and income growth underpinned the economy in the first half of the year and that looks to remains the case in 2012 Q3.

relations.evidence: The value of imports in US$ terms grew by 4.7% compared to 6.2% the prior month, while volume growth picked up to 8.8%. The trend in the growth rate of import volumes is upward and suggests that domestic demand is strengthening.

Shipments to the developed economies led the deceleration, with those to the US slowing from growth of 10.6% year-on-year to 0.6% and those to the EU contracting by 16%. After allowing for exchange rate and price effects, export volumes showed no growth compared to a year earlier.

chunk.text: growth. Household consumption and income growth underpinned the economy in the first half of the year and that looks to remains the case in 2012 Q3. Retail sales, an albeit imperfect measure of consumer spending, grew by 12.2% year-on-year last month in real terms according to official data, the fastest rate of increase this year. The downtrend in inflation since the summer of last year has boosted real spending power against the backdrop of a relatively tight labour market. To date there have been few signs of the labour market easing but should they emerge they would be a downside signal for the economic outlook and would likely prompt more aggressive policy easing. While consumption is providing stability to the economy, Fixed Asset Investment (FAI) is the key to the recovery. The real rate of growth dipped in last month, but growth in the three months to July was the fastest since the closing stages of 2010. Infrastructure investment is in the vanguard, with transport infrastructure now coming to the fore. Investment by the manufacturing industry is also strengthening, despite relatively sluggish growth in output and squeeze on profits that the sector is experiencing.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A 141 CRU International Limited confidential the official Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) and from anecdotal evidence, which indicate excess inventories of both finished goods and raw materials. We have reduced our industrial production forecast for this year to 10.4% due to the weakness of incoming data. Worryingly, the just released August ‘flash’ PMI from HSBC/Markit fell back to 47.8, its lowest reading since November. The survey reports that new orders deteriorated and finished goods inventories rose further, pointing to further downside risk to the outlook. For the time being our GDP forecast for this year is unchanged at 8.0%, with growth of 7.9% expected this quarter. However, if the official data for August do not show an improvement then we will likely revise our view lower. We expect the government to ease policy further given both the headwinds being faced by the real economy and the favourable inflation backdrop.

chunk.text: he manufacturing industry is also strengthening, despite relatively sluggish growth in output and squeeze on profits that the sector is experiencing. Real estate remains on a drag on investment and this is likely to be the case during the rest of the year. Policy easing has allowed a recovery in sales since the spring which has gathered pace in the past three months such that July saw floor area sold rise by 13.3% year-on-year and property prices are rising. However, excess inventories are clearly not being whittled down quickly enough yet for developers, who slashed the amount of new floor area started by 26.7% year-on-year last month – a result consistent with expectations for real estate starts to be cut by 15% for the year as a whole. The picture for completions is currently a more positive one as they increased by 9.4% year-on-year in July due to the backlog of previously started projects being worked through. With the lag between starts and completions typically around two years, the reductions in floor area started this year will not be fully felt until 2014.

Growth of industrial production was particularly disappointing last month as it dropped to 9.2% year-on-year from 9.5% in June. While growth of the consumer and export driven light industrial sector accelerated to 10.1% from 9.0% the prior month, heavy industry slowed from 9.6% to a three-year low of 8.8%. Heavy industry is typically more cyclical than light industry due to its relative exposure to the cyclical elements of demand, such as investment and the inventory cycle. chunk.text: ower. We expect the government to ease policy further given both the headwinds being faced by the real economy and the favourable inflation backdrop. Headline inflation fell to its lowest rate for two and a half years last month and, with officials re-stating their commitment to stimulating growth, reductions in interest rate and banks’ required reserve ratios are likely in the short term. We continue to expect a rebound in growth to over 8.5% in 2013 as the benefits of this year’s policy easing are fully felt, although this view is contingent on the Eurozone avoiding collapse. Beyond 2013 we forecast that sub-8% GDP growth will become the norm as the economy rebalances away from its reliance on investment, industry and profits in favour of consumption, services and wages. This year provides a foretaste of the China to come. 2.3.2 India and East Asia: Lack of reforms hitting India hard Economic activity in India remained soft in the quarter ending in June and high frequency data suggest that growth will be below trend in the second half of this year as well.

chunk.text: remained soft in the quarter ending in June and high frequency data suggest that growth will be below trend in the second half of this year as well. Weak external demand, in particular from Europe and the USA, has limited exports but a lack of economic reforms and a gaping fiscal deficit have hit the economy the hardest. In signs that the Indian economy is faltering, manufacturing, which constitutes around 75% of industrial production, shrank in June by 3.2% year-on-year. And for the quarter ending in June, industrial activity dropped by 0.1% quarter-on-quarter against almost 7% growth in the same period in 2011. There are base effects at work but the figures still imply a significant slowdown. The 27% year-on-year drop in output of capital goods in June, for example, was the steepest plunge on record. What’s more, other leading indicators aren’t particularly encouraging either. The Markit manufacturing PMI, for example, fell significantly in July to a reading of 52.9, from 55 the previous month. The moderation in activity was on the back of power outages that hit large parts of northern India and a drop in new export orders.

chunk.text: e previous month. The moderation in activity was on the back of power outages that hit large parts of northern India and a drop in new export orders. Indeed, exports fell by almost 3% year- on-year in the second quarter and external demand is set to remain weak. Wholesale price inflation fell in July to 6.9% from 7.3% a month earlier, the lowest level for almost three years but still at elevated levels. However, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) wants to see further evidence of easing inflationary pressures before it eases policy again and held the benchmark rate at 8% in its July meeting. In our opinion, while the poor external environment is contributing to the slowdown, it is ineffectual political leadership which continues to damage confidence, discourage investment

chunk.text: 142 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A CRU International Limited confidential and now likely responsible for several years of below-potential economic growth in India. We have become increasingly concerned about the scope of the country’s leaders to restore the economy onto the path of high growth rates and have cut our forecasts again this month. In terms of industrial production, our outlook is for 2.7% growth this year before rebounding to grow by 7.7% in 2013. We now expect GDP growth of 6% this year, followed by 7.4% in 2013 (down from 6.3% and 7.6% last month). Malaysia’s economy surpassed expectations in 2012 Q2. Growth picked up by 5.4% year- on-year with strong domestic demand more than compensating for weakness in exports. While household spending has been strong due to a resilient labour market, industrial production has also been trending up. As a result, we’ve revised up our industrial production growth forecast for this year to 3.8%. Overall, we expect weak external demand to weigh on the economy during the remainder of the year and maintain our forecast of 4.3% growth this year and 4.6% in 2013.

chunk.text: ct weak external demand to weigh on the economy during the remainder of the year and maintain our forecast of 4.3% growth this year and 4.6% in 2013. Second-quarter GDP growth of 6.4% year-on-year in Indonesia emphasises how well the economy is performing despite the slowdown in global export markets. In the three months to June, export growth slowed to 1.9% year-on-year (from 7.8% in the previous period) but since the economy is relatively closed compared to a number of its neighbours the impact is relatively smaller. What’s more the economy remains able to rely on robust domestic demand, expanding by 5% year-on-year. For these reasons we expect GDP growth to average 6% this year, followed by around 6.4% next year. 2.3.3 Japan: Government support still primary growth driver Japan’s economy barely grew at all in 2012 Q2, expanding by just 0.3% quarter-on-quarter (1.4% annualised). As in 2012 Q1, government support remained the primary driver of economic growth (contributing 0.2 percentage points to the quarterly growth rate).

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix A 143 CRU International Limited confidential consumption growth all but slowed to a halt. In the first three month, consumer spending, on the back of government subsidies for fuel efficient cars, rose by 1.2% quarter-on-quarter but grew by only 0.1% in 2012 Q2. The subsidy is likely to run out this summer and we expect consumer spending to fall sharply in 2012 Q3. Meanwhile, the strongest contribution to growth came from reconstruction-related public investment. Rubble clearance and decontamination projects meant that public spending rose by 1.7% quarter-on-quarter. However, the amount of additional stimulus measures is fading each quarter and with it the contribution it brings to economic growth. Industry has continued to be a drain on the economy, contracting in every month of the second quarter and into the third. Industrial production fell in July by 0.1% following a 25% year-on-year collapse in sales to the EU and 12% fall in sales to China. Business conditions in Japan’s manufacturing sector, as measured by survey data, deteriorated in July.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 CRU International Limited confidential Appendix B Interest and exchange rate comments QE3 puts downward pressure on US dollar The US Federal Reserve’s new programme of quantitative easing enacted in mid-September (QE3) has renewed pressure on the US dollar, even as the European economic landscape remains in a constant state of convulsion. The single currency reached a four-month high in mid-September. However, some of these gains can also be attributed to recent intervention by the European Central Bank (ECB). The Japanese yen continues to be treated as a preferred safe-haven asset in times of global financial market stress. Against the euro, the yen has been by far the world’s best-performing currency over the past five years. However, the fundamental outlook for the yen is negative, as aggressive monetary policy, an anticipated deceleration in growth and demographics weigh on the currency.

chunk.text: 160 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix B CRU International Limited confidential Central banks on the offensive Recent weeks have seen the world’s major central banks launch action to revive the global economy. On September 6, the European Central Bank outlined a new bond-buying program, letting markets know that there were no pre-set limits to its purchases. On September 13, the United States Federal Reserve announced that in the coming months it would purchase some $85 billion of long-term securities per month, with the aim of putting downward pressure on long-term interest rates and supporting growth. Finally, on September 19, the Bank of Japan declared that it was adding another ¥10 trillion ($128 billion) to its government securities purchase program, and that it expected its total holdings of such paper to reach about $1 trillion by end-2013. • At its mid September meeting, the Federal Reserve announced an open-ended programme of quantitative easing (QE3) focused on mortgage-backed securities (MBS) purchases and extended its conditional commitment to leave its policy rate at near-zero until mid-2015.

relations.evidence: At its mid September meeting, the Federal Reserve announced an open-ended programme of quantitative easing (QE3) focused on mortgage-backed securities (MBS) purchases

chunk.text: ) focused on mortgage-backed securities (MBS) purchases and extended its conditional commitment to leave its policy rate at near-zero until mid-2015. There were numerous other tweaks to its communications strategy, most of which involved shifting the focus of policy away from fixed calendar dates and stressing more that future policy action will depend on how economic conditions develop. The Fed pledged to make $40bn of MBS purchases per month (plus $45bn worth of purchases as part of its ongoing “operation twist”) and to continue those purchases until it judges that the outlook for the labour market has improved substantially. Overall, we are doubtful about the impact of QE3 on the real economy, and expect the Fed to increase its purchases sometime in 2013, perhaps re-focusing on treasuries. • At its July meeting, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut its main interest rate to a historic low of 0.75% from 1% in mid-July amid signs that prospects for the Eurozone economy are looking increasingly bleak. Furthermore, at its September meeting it announced a new large scale bond purchase programme aimed at soothing jittery bond markets.

chunk.text: reasingly bleak. Furthermore, at its September meeting it announced a new large scale bond purchase programme aimed at soothing jittery bond markets. Still, as expected, bond purchases will not be a blank cheque to Eurozone governments. Conditions will be attached, which the IMF will be asked to set and monitor. Annual Eurozone inflation accelerated to 2.6% in August from 2.4% in July, coming in stronger than expected and further above the ECB's target of just below 2%. Looking ahead, the bank is unlikely to reduce interest rates further as confidence in the region stabilises and inflation remains above target. • In early September, the Bank of England’s Monetary Policy Committee voted to hold rates at their current record low of 0.5% and leave the targeted size of the quantitative easing (QE) programme at £375bn. Meanwhile, rising food and energy costs have revived near-term inflationary concerns. The chances for additional policy easing by the Bank have receded, although we consider the door still open for expanded asset purchases of £50bn at the November meeting.

The BoJ, limited in its ability given interest rates are already near zero, instead increased the size of the asset purchase programme, by buying another ¥10 trn of government bonds, to a total of ¥80 trn. The additional round of quantitative easing was regarded by analysts as retaliation against the Fed’s announcement but did little to stem the period of appreciation of the currency over the last month. • In China, the People’s Bank (PBoC) followed up its surprise June interest rate cut with another reduction less than a month later in early July. The benchmark one-year lending and savings rates now stand at 6.00% and 3.00%, respectively. The economy appears to be stabilising but a pick-up in growth has yet to materialise, suggesting that additional policy easing is required.

chunk.text: ectively. The economy appears to be stabilising but a pick-up in growth has yet to materialise, suggesting that additional policy easing is required. However, the window for further rate cuts is closing as inflation has bottomed out and should begin to rise from here as favourable base effects drop out of the year-on-year comparison and as food price inflation rises once again. We see scope for one further rate cut at most in this cycle and forecast rates to rise in the second half of next year due to higher inflation and a stronger economy. In the medium term we expect that interest rates will typically be higher than the experience of the past decade as the authorities aim to normalise rates as part of their efforts to rebalance the economy. • Since our last update, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has cut the repo rate by 50bp to 8.0%. However, it’s likely that the RBI is reluctant to cut the benchmark lending rate much further in the near future due to persistently high inflation, despite calls from industry complaining that high borrowing costs have stifled investment in infrastructure and manufacturing.

chunk.text: tently high inflation, despite calls from industry complaining that high borrowing costs have stifled investment in infrastructure and manufacturing. The WPI inflation measure rose from 6.9% in July to 7.6% in August, reversing the disinflation achieved since the end of last year and is likely to weigh on future decisions to cut rates. That said, while the RBI has little scope to ease monetary policy there is now a slightly higher probability that it will cut the repo rate in October in support of new reforms to liberalise the Indian economy. The RBI will, however, probably maintain the key interest rate above 7.5% by the end of this year, as inflation is likely to come under further upward pressure from food and fuel prices. We anticipate that looser monetary policy will likely begin next year as inflation falls back. • Though inflationary pressures have moderated in South Africa (at 4.9% y/y in July, the headline rate is now well within the official target range), monetary authorities will be kept on alert going forward, given the weakened trajectory of the rand and possible pass through from rising global food prices.

Chief among the longer-term issues of concern to the South African Reserve Bank (SARB) are high structural unemployment, inadequate electricity provision and feeble private investment. The twin fiscal and current account deficits also continue to generate unease among investors. • In Australia, inflation is currently low due to the strong Australian dollar, but is expected to rise as a result of the newly-introduced carbon tax, before returning to the middle of the 2- 3% target range by end-2013.
