Indeed, we now expect Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) to cut interest rates at its next meeting in early October. • Unprecedented policy action from the Brazilian central bank with interest rate cuts for exactly a year in August now looks as though it might be coming to an end. In efforts to reignite the stalling Brazilian economy, the central bank has cut the benchmark Selic rate to an all-time low of 7.5% and we expect the lower interest rate to contribute to boosting the economy in 2013. However, the slight month-on-month rise in the rate of inflation in September to 5.3% (a rate above the target rate of 4.5% but within the tolerance level of plus or minus two percentage points) means that the central bank is likely to pause in its aggressive cycle of monetary easing or make only a small cut in the final quarter of this year. As the economy accelerates next year our outlook is for the central bank to raise interest rates in the second half in order to keep inflation in check.

chunk.text: Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix B 163 CRU International Limited confidential More exchange rate volatility In our forecasts short to medium-term parities are influenced by market-making factors, such as comparative interest rates, which are of a cyclical nature, and inflation expectations, trade deficits and relative growth rates, which are of a more structural nature. Increasingly, however, some of these structural influences may act in opposition to cyclical influences. For instance, a currency may not weaken much in response to cuts in interest rates that are made mainly to support growth. In the long term, however, currencies are projected to revert towards their `fair’ values, which are closely akin to the values implied by purchasing power parities. In other words currencies are forecast to revert to cross-parities that compensate for the varying prices, in local currency terms, of a common basket of goods. • The US trade-weighted dollar has been under pressure over the summer months.

chunk.text: the economy accelerates next year our outlook is for the central bank to raise interest rates in the second half in order to keep inflation in check. • Meanwhile in Russia, inflationary pressures have intensified in recent months on the back of the drought which has affected this year’s grain harvest, as well as the implementation of regulated price and tariff increases. In August the consumer price index grew 0.1% over the prior month, with the annual inflation rising to 5.9% y/y from 5.6% in July. As a consequence the central bank of Russia raised its refinancing rate by 25 basis points to 8.25%. Looking ahead, we do not anticipate any further major monetary policy changes in the next six months; however, upside inflation risks will be monitored closely.

The currency may be on the defensive as monetary authorities remain committed to maintaining a near-zero short-term interest rate environment and to its large-scale asset (US Treasury and Mortgage-based securities) purchase program (QE3) announced in mid-September aimed at depressing long-term funding costs for both the government and mortgage borrowers. The US fiscal outlook is complicated by the November election; however, market participants will demand clarity on key government policies before year-end. • The euro has gained from improved market risk-appetite in recent weeks, after the European Central Bank laid out plans to buy government bonds in an effort to stem the Eurozone debt crisis, and the Federal Reserve enacted its QE3 program. As a result, the single currency hit a four-month high of €1.31 in mid-September. However, the European economic landscape remains in a constant state of convulsion.

chunk.text: single currency hit a four-month high of €1.31 in mid-September. However, the European economic landscape remains in a constant state of convulsion. Despite a temporary relief in the selloff spree, the underlying structural weakness of the peripheral economies, coupled with the governance challenges facing German and French leaders, should keep the euro on the defensive for a prolonged period. We now expect the single currency to close the year at around $1.25. • Since the end of the second quarter the Japanese yen has strengthened slightly, remaining below ¥80, driven by safe-haven currency perceptions and more recently the Fed announcement of the third round of quantitative easing. The BoJ responded to the Fed’s decision by increasing the size of its asset purchase programme with the effect that the yen immediately weakened but by close of play on the day of the announcement had returned to the level at which it had started. We believe that in an environment where all the major central banks are easing monetary policy to support growth further yen gains are limited.

rted. We believe that in an environment where all the major central banks are easing monetary policy to support growth further yen gains are limited. We therefore have maintained out outlook for the currency to weaken from the beginning of this year to end it at around ¥79 to the dollar. • The British pound has performed well over the summer supported by its triple-A rating. The bullish outlook for the British pound is temporarily damped as the UK struggles to meet its austerity plan and to address the economic contraction. The Bank of England maintains aggressive policy as the UK finds itself highly exposed to the European crisis; however, on a relative basis the currency should fare better than euro and perform strongly against the US dollar in 2013.

164 Precious Metals Quarterly 2012 Q3 Appendix B CRU International Limited confidential • Australia’s enviable sovereign credit position, interest rate differentials and strong foreign demand for high-yield currencies drove up the value of Australian dollar to a five-month high of AUD$0.94 against the US dollar in early August. However, loosening monetary policy, the currency’s high risk status and fears concerning the end of the Australian mining boom weigh heavily on what would otherwise be a bullish outlook. We now expect the currency to close the year at around AUD$0.98. In the longer term it should also shed some value to correct the current account imbalance. • After a period of steady depreciation which began in early February and lasted up until mid- September, the Indian rupee has held steady at around 55.5 to the dollar. However following the long-awaited announcement of government reform measures, including cutting diesel subsidies and the relaxation of limits on foreign direct investment, the rupee hit its strongest level in four months reaching around Rs53, buoyed by a marked improvement in sentiment.

its on foreign direct investment, the rupee hit its strongest level in four months reaching around Rs53, buoyed by a marked improvement in sentiment. While we believe that the reforms are a step in the right direction we take note that they have yet to be implemented and given the willingness of the government to reverse legislation we expect the rupee will struggle to appreciate much more this year and our forecast is for the currency to end the year at around 54.6 to the dollar. • The South African rand continues to fluctuate in a wide trading range around 8.3 per US dollar. The path of the currency depends to a large degree on changes in the global risk environment, particularly with respect to the euro crisis and commodity prices. Over the medium term, the currency is likely to remain constrained by South Africa’s ongoing political uncertainty, social tensions and considerable twin deficits. • The Chinese RMB reached a low point of 6.39 to the US$ in late July but, despite the deterioration in China’s economic outlook since then, the currency has strengthened to 6.31.

We expect limited gains for the RMB over the next six months as the long-awaited economic rebound emerges more gradually than expected. Thereafter we project that the RMB will appreciate by 1.5-3.5%pa into the medium term. • The Brazilian real has been one of the world’s worst performing currencies for most of this year, with President Rousseff complaining that a “tsunami” of hot money caused by monetary easing in the developed world was ruining the competitiveness of Brazilian industry. The real depreciated steadily at the beginning of the year and since mid-May has remained at or around 2 reals to the dollar. The central bank intervened in mid-year to prevent further strengthening and attempting to contain inflation.

With suggestions from the central bank that there would be no further cuts in the benchmark Selic rate this year, the possibility of new appreciation pressure on the currency has become a distinct possibility. We expect a period of appreciation between now and mid-2013 with the currency reaching around 1.94 to the dollar by the year end. • The Russian ruble has benefitted from the recovery in oil prices since late June, though not to the same extent as was seen in the early part of the year. The currency remains constrained, however, by persistent capital outflows and the still fragile global risk environment. We expect the ruble to end the year at around 32 against the US dollar.
